whenGood.example: After a change to checkout logic, call /verify to observe whether the flow really works in real operation.

whenBad.title: The diff only touches tests or docs
whenBad.why: In that case there's no runtime surface to observe.
whenBad.alternative: Run the regular test suite — no /verify needed.

commands.name: /vim
commands.summary: No longer exists — removed in version 2.1.92.
commands.what:
No longer exists — removed in version 2.1.92.

Switch Vim mode via /config → Editor mode instead. The key bindings themselves stayed; only the dedicated command for them is gone.

whenGood.title: Nothing — the command is gone
whenGood.example: Run /config and switch between Vim and Normal under "Editor mode".

whenBad.title: On version 2.1.92 or later
whenBad.why: the command was removed in 2.1.92 and does nothing
whenBad.alternative: Use /config → Editor mode

commands.name: /model
commands.summary: Switches the AI model used in the running Codex session.
commands.what: /model opens the model picker within a running interactive Codex session. This lets you switch the model without restarting the session. When starting Codex, the model can alternatively be set directly with the --model or -m flags.

whenGood.title: Upgrade the model for a harder subtask
whenGood.example: /model — then pick a more capable model before tackling a complex refactor

whenGood.title: Faster/cheaper model for routine tasks
whenGood.example: /model — switch to a smaller model to handle formatting or text tasks more cheaply

whenBad.title: The model should already be fixed at startup
whenBad.why: If you already know which model you'll need for the whole session, there's no need to switch into the session and toggle it there.
whenBad.alternative: Set the model directly at startup with --model or -m, e.g. codex --model o3

commands.name: /permissions
commands.summary: Controls which actions Codex may perform without asking (approval mode).
commands.what: /permissions is the successor to /approvals and controls the approval mode of the running session. It sets how much Codex may do on its own before asking for confirmation — comparable to the startup flags --ask-for-approval/--approval-mode and --sandbox (e.g. workspace-write or danger-full-access).

whenGood.title: Temporarily grant more freedom for riskier steps
whenGood.example: /permissions — loosen the sandbox/approval mode before Codex needs to install dependencies or run migrations

whenGood.title: Restrict again after a sensitive action
whenGood.example: /permissions — switch back to a stricter mode after Codex has had filesystem access

whenBad.title: Approvals should be fixed for the whole session from the start
whenBad.why: Manually switching mid-session is unnecessary if the desired mode is already clear before starting.
whenBad.alternative: Set the mode directly at startup, e.g. with --full-auto or --sandbox workspace-write

commands.name: /status
commands.summary: Shows the current status of the running Codex session.
commands.what: /status gives an overview of the running interactive session. It's part of the official list of Codex CLI slash commands used to check the state of the session instead of blindly changing settings like model or approval mode.

whenGood.title: Check which mode you're in before an important action
whenGood.example: /status — take a quick look before Codex is about to make changes to the repository

whenGood.title: Re-orient yourself after a longer break
whenGood.example: /status — get an overview when resuming work after an interruption

whenBad.title: You want to actively change something, not just check
whenBad.why: /status only displays state, it doesn't change anything.
whenBad.alternative: Use the appropriate commands for changes, e.g. /model or /permissions

commands.name: /plan
commands.summary: Shows or manages the work plan for the current task.
commands.what: /plan is part of the official list of interactive Codex slash commands and refers to the plan for the task running within the session. It complements commands like /status and /goal, which let you track the goal and progress of a session.

whenGood.title: Overview of the planned steps before a larger task
whenGood.example: /plan — see how Codex intends to approach a multi-step task before approving it

whenGood.title: Check mid-task whether the plan still fits the task
whenGood.example: /plan — check during an ongoing implementation whether the next steps still make sense

whenBad.title: Task is trivial and single-step
whenBad.why: For a simple one-step change, looking at the plan adds little value.
whenBad.alternative: Just state the task directly and let Codex execute it

commands.name: /review
commands.summary: Triggers a review of the current code changes.
commands.what: /review is part of the official list of Codex CLI's interactive slash commands. It lives in the same namespace as the CLI subcommand codex review, which can review changes with flags like --uncommitted, --base, --commit, or --title. Within a running session it lets you trigger a review of the changes made so far.

whenGood.title: Double-check your own changes before committing
whenGood.example: /review — have the current, not-yet-committed changes looked over

whenGood.title: Request a second look before a merge
whenGood.example: /review — have the session's changes checked before merging into the main branch

whenBad.title: The review should run outside an interactive session, e.g. in CI
whenBad.why: /review requires a running interactive session.
whenBad.alternative: Use the standalone codex review subcommand with flags like --base or --commit

commands.name: /diff
commands.summary: Shows the current changes (diff) of the session.
commands.what: /diff is part of the official list of interactive Codex slash commands and shows the changes Codex has made or proposed in the running session. It complements commands like /review, which additionally evaluate the changes.

whenGood.title: Quickly check what Codex has changed so far
whenGood.example: /diff — after a few work steps, check which files were affected

whenGood.title: See the exact line changes before approving
whenGood.example: /diff — look at the changes before letting Codex apply them

whenBad.title: You need a substantive assessment, not just the raw diff text
whenBad.why: /diff shows the changes but doesn't evaluate them.
whenBad.alternative: Use /review to additionally have the changes assessed

commands.name: /resume
commands.summary: Continues an earlier Codex session.
commands.what: /resume is part of the official list of interactive slash commands and matches the purpose of the CLI subcommand resume, which lets you continue sessions, including via resume --last or a specific session ID. Within the TUI, /resume lets you return to a previous session instead of starting fresh.

whenGood.title: Continue the same task after an interruption
whenGood.example: /resume — go back to the last session to keep working there

whenGood.title: Deliberately resume a specific earlier session
whenGood.example: /resume — pick the right one from a list of previous sessions

whenBad.title: You deliberately want to start completely fresh
whenBad.why: /resume picks up an existing session along with its context, which isn't wanted here.
whenBad.alternative: Use /new for a fresh session with no prior context

commands.name: /new
commands.summary: Starts a new, empty Codex session.
commands.what: /new is part of the official list of interactive Codex slash commands and starts a new session within the TUI. It's the opposite of /resume, which continues an existing session, and of /fork, which branches an existing session.

whenGood.title: Start a new, independent topic
whenGood.example: /new — start a fresh session for a task unrelated to the previous one

whenGood.title: Session is cluttered with old context
whenGood.example: /new — start over when the prior session history is no longer relevant

whenBad.title: The session's existing context is still needed
whenBad.why: /new discards the session's existing context.
whenBad.alternative: Use /resume to keep working on the existing session, or /fork to branch off a variant of it

commands.name: /fork
commands.summary: Branches the current session into a new, independent copy.
commands.what: /fork is part of the official list of interactive slash commands and serves the same purpose as the CLI subcommand of the same name, which belongs to session management alongside archive/unarchive/delete. It lets you use the session history so far as the starting point for a new, separate session.

whenGood.title: Try two different approaches from the same starting point
whenGood.example: /fork — branch off from the current session state to test an alternative approach without altering the original session

whenGood.title: Test a risky next step in isolation
whenGood.example: /fork — create a copy of the session before a larger change

whenBad.title: There should only be a single, linear session
whenBad.why: /fork creates an additional, separate session copy, which is unnecessary overhead here.
whenBad.alternative: Simply keep working in the existing session

commands.name: /mcp
commands.summary: Shows or manages the MCP servers connected to Codex.
commands.what: /mcp is part of the official list of interactive slash commands and corresponds to the CLI subcommands mcp and mcp-server, used to configure MCP servers. Within the session, /mcp gives access to the connected MCP servers.

whenGood.title: Check which MCP servers are currently available
whenGood.example: /mcp — see which external tools/servers are available to the session

whenGood.title: Verify access before a task that needs external tools
whenGood.example: /mcp — check whether a required MCP server is connected before starting a task that needs it

whenBad.title: It's only about local file/code changes with no external tools
whenBad.why: Without a need for MCP servers, /mcp adds no value for the task.
whenBad.alternative: Proceed directly with the actual task without touching MCP configuration

commands.name: /skills
commands.summary: Shows the skills available for the session.
commands.what: /skills is part of the official list of interactive Codex slash commands. It sits alongside /plugins in session controls; the exact details aren't further documented.

whenGood.title: Check available skills before a specialized task
whenGood.example: /skills — see which skills can be used for the upcoming task

whenGood.title: Get an overview when unsure what Codex can additionally do
whenGood.example: /skills — check the list before formulating a task that needs a special approach

whenBad.title: Task is trivial and needs no special skills
whenBad.why: For a simple change, checking available skills is an unnecessary extra step.
whenBad.alternative: Just state the task directly

commands.name: /goal
commands.summary: Sets a persistent task goal that Codex works toward across the session.
commands.what: According to the official Codex CLI docs, /goal sets a persistent task goal. Unlike a one-off instruction, this goal persists across the session and guides Codex.

whenGood.title: Set an overarching goal for a longer work session
whenGood.example: /goal — e.g. set 'fix all open type errors in the project' as an ongoing goal

whenGood.title: Keep focus across multiple individual steps
whenGood.example: /goal — set a goal so Codex doesn't lose track across several consecutive instructions

whenBad.title: The task is a permanently recurring routine, not a one-off goal
whenBad.why: /goal is meant for a goal within a session, not for recurring, time-triggered workflows.
whenBad.alternative: Set up a loop/automation for permanently recurring tasks instead of a one-off /goal

commands.name: codex exec
commands.summary: Runs Codex non-interactively, e.g. for scripts or CI.
commands.what: codex exec (short form: e) runs a task non-interactively without opening the TUI. You can also pass '-' as the prompt to read input from stdin. With --json, events are output as JSONL; with -o/--output-last-message, only the last message is saved; and with --output-schema, an output schema is enforced.

whenGood.title: Integrate Codex into a script or pipeline
whenGood.example: codex exec "summarize the changes from the last commits" --json

whenGood.title: Pipe input from another source via stdin
whenGood.example: cat task.txt | codex exec -

whenBad.title: You want to steer interactively, e.g. answer follow-up questions live
whenBad.why: codex exec runs through without interactive follow-up questions.
whenBad.alternative: Start the normal TUI with codex (without exec)

commands.name: codex review
commands.summary: Runs a review of changes via the CLI.
commands.what: codex review reviews code changes and supports the flags --uncommitted, --base, --commit, and --title to determine which changes are considered and how the review is titled.

whenGood.title: Review not-yet-committed changes before committing
whenGood.example: codex review --uncommitted

whenGood.title: Review changes against a specific branch
whenGood.example: codex review --base main

whenBad.title: The review should be part of a running interactive session
whenBad.why: codex review is a standalone CLI invocation outside a running TUI session.
whenBad.alternative: Use /review instead within a running session

commands.name: codex resume
commands.summary: Continues an earlier Codex session via the CLI.
commands.what: codex resume continues a previous session, including via resume --last for the most recently used session or with a specific session ID. The same resume logic also exists as exec resume --last/<ID> for non-interactive mode.

whenGood.title: Continue the last session directly from the command line
whenGood.example: codex resume --last

whenGood.title: Specifically continue a particular earlier session
whenGood.example: codex resume <ID>

whenBad.title: You're already in a running interactive session
whenBad.why: For resuming within the TUI, the standalone CLI call is unnecessary.
whenBad.alternative: Use /resume within the session

commands.name: codex apply
commands.summary: Applies a diff created in the cloud locally.
commands.what: codex apply takes a diff produced e.g. by a cloud task (cloud/cloud exec/cloud list) and applies it locally in the working directory.

whenGood.title: Apply the result of a cloud task locally
whenGood.example: codex apply <task-id> — apply the diff generated in the cloud locally

whenGood.title: Check a cloud result before applying it locally
whenGood.example: codex cloud list, then codex apply for the desired task

whenBad.title: There's no cloud diff at all, only local changes
whenBad.why: codex apply is meant for applying cloud diffs locally, not for purely local changes.
whenBad.alternative: Make changes directly locally with the normal Codex session

commands.name: /model
commands.summary: Switches the active AI model in the running CLI session.
commands.what: /model opens the model picker (fuzzy search via [filter]) inside a running Cursor Agent session. Press Tab to edit the selected entry. Changes to Fast mode, reasoning effort, or context are preserved across a model switch. The model can also be set at startup with --model.

whenGood.title: Upgrade for a difficult task
whenGood.example: /model opus — before a complex refactor spanning multiple files

whenGood.title: Pick a faster model for routine work
whenGood.example: /model — switch to a cheaper model to, e.g., write commit messages

whenBad.title: The model should already be fixed at startup
whenBad.why: If you already know which model you need, there's no need to switch into the session and toggle it there.
whenBad.alternative: Set the model directly with a flag, e.g. agent --model opus

whenBad.title: Just jumping between known model families quickly
whenBad.why: The full fuzzy search of /model is more effort than needed for a quick jump to a known model.
whenBad.alternative: Use a model shortcut like /opus or /composer

commands.name: /run-everything
commands.summary: Toggles 'Run Everything' autopilot mode, which runs commands without asking for approval.
commands.what: /run-everything (alias /auto-run) toggles Run Everything on/off, or shows the current state with status. In this mode the agent runs commands without individual approval prompts.

whenGood.title: Many repetitive, low-risk steps in a row
whenGood.example: /run-everything on — e.g. for a long chain of lint fixes across many files

whenGood.title: Check the current autopilot state before a task
whenGood.example: /run-everything status — quick check before riskier steps come up

whenBad.title: Production or destructive commands are coming up
whenBad.why: Without prompts, the agent can accidentally delete data or change production systems.
whenBad.alternative: Use /sandbox or /auto-review instead for controlled approvals

whenBad.title: Only a single risky command needs to go through
whenBad.why: Turning on the full autopilot for that opens up more than needed.
whenBad.alternative: Just approve the single prompt normally instead of granting blanket approval

commands.name: /plan
commands.summary: Switches to Plan mode, where the agent drafts a plan before acting.
commands.what: /plan switches to Plan mode, shows the current plan, or submits a prompt directly in Plan mode. Plan mode has a persistent plan menu with 'Build Locally' and 'Build in Cloud' options; plans are saved to disk and can be transferred to cloud agents.

whenGood.title: Nail down the strategy before a bigger rework
whenGood.example: /plan Migrate the auth system to OAuth2 — review the plan before touching code

whenGood.title: Structure a multi-step task before the agent starts
whenGood.example: /plan — view and adjust the current plan before picking 'Build Locally'

whenBad.title: Just a short, unambiguous single change
whenBad.why: For a simple one-line change, going through a plan first is unnecessary.
whenBad.alternative: Prompt directly in the normal agent mode

whenBad.title: You just want to read and understand, not plan
whenBad.why: /plan targets execution planning, not pure research without intent to implement.
whenBad.alternative: Use /ask for read-only questions

commands.name: /ask
commands.summary: Toggles Ask mode, where the agent only reads and makes no changes.
commands.what: /ask toggles Ask mode for read-only questions. In Ask mode the agent can explore code and answer questions but cannot modify files or run commands with side effects. Also startable directly with --mode ask.

whenGood.title: Understand a codebase before planning changes
whenGood.example: /ask — then ask 'How is the auth middleware structured?'

whenGood.title: Make sure nothing gets changed by accident
whenGood.example: /ask — research in an unfamiliar repo with no write risk

whenBad.title: The agent actually needs to change code
whenBad.why: Changes are blocked in Ask mode, so the task doesn't move forward.
whenBad.alternative: Switch back to normal agent mode, or use /plan for structured implementation

whenBad.title: A multi-step plan needs to be produced
whenBad.why: /ask only gives answers, not step-by-step planning.
whenBad.alternative: Use /plan

commands.name: /debug
commands.summary: Toggles Debug mode, built for reproducing and narrowing down bugs.
commands.what: /debug toggles Debug mode, or submits a prompt directly in Debug mode. Reproduction-step cards are shown with their own (red) accent in Debug mode, so the active mode stays clear.

whenGood.title: Narrow down a specific bug
whenGood.example: /debug Login fails after a password reset

whenGood.title: Work through reproduction steps systematically
whenGood.example: /debug — activate the mode before pasting a bug report with a stack trace

whenBad.title: A new feature should be built directly
whenBad.why: Debug mode is built for bug-finding and narrowing, not new development.
whenBad.alternative: Use normal agent mode or /plan for feature work

whenBad.title: The bug is already clearly located
whenBad.why: If the cause and the fix are already clear, Debug mode adds no value.
whenBad.alternative: Ask for the fix directly in agent mode

commands.name: /logs
commands.summary: Shows the path to the current debug log file and copies it to the clipboard.
commands.what: /logs shows the debug log file path for the running session and copies it to the clipboard. Debug logs are written continuously for every session.

whenGood.title: Prepare a support request with a log file
whenGood.example: /logs — copy the path and attach it to a support ticket

whenBad.title: You just need the last request ID, not the full log file
whenBad.why: /logs gives you a file path, not a single ID.
whenBad.alternative: Use /copy-request-id for the last request ID

commands.name: /update
commands.summary: Updates Cursor Agent to the latest version directly from the running session.
commands.what: /update updates the CLI in place without leaving the session. The same thing also works via agent update in the terminal.

whenGood.title: Want to use a new feature from the changelog
whenGood.example: /update — then restart the session to load the new version

whenBad.title: In the middle of a critical, running task
whenBad.why: Updating mid-task can disturb the state of the current session.
whenBad.alternative: Push the update to after the task is done, or run agent update separately in the terminal

commands.name: /max-mode
commands.summary: Toggles 'Max Mode' for the currently selected model, if supported.
commands.what: /max-mode toggles Max Mode for the currently selected model. The state is shown in the footer and persists across sessions.

whenGood.title: Maximum model capability for a complex task
whenGood.example: /max-mode — turn on before a large architecture analysis

whenBad.title: The current model doesn't support Max Mode
whenBad.why: The toggle only has an effect on models that offer Max Mode.
whenBad.alternative: First switch to a Max-Mode-capable model with /model

commands.name: /rename
commands.summary: Renames the current chat session.
commands.what: /rename gives the current chat session a new name.

whenGood.title: Make several parallel sessions distinguishable
whenGood.example: /rename Auth-Refactor — before switching between multiple open chats

whenBad.title: Just a single, short-lived session
whenBad.why: For a throwaway chat, renaming usually isn't worth it.
whenBad.alternative: Keep the default name, find it later with /resume if needed

commands.name: /clear
commands.summary: Starts a new, empty chat session.
commands.what: /clear starts a new chat session. /new, /new-chat, and /newchat are aliases.

whenGood.title: Free up the context window for a completely new topic
whenGood.example: /clear — after the previous task is done

whenBad.title: The prior context is still needed
whenBad.why: /clear discards the current session's conversation history entirely.
whenBad.alternative: Use /summarize to free up space without losing the context completely

commands.name: /resume
commands.summary: Opens a list of recent chats across all workspaces and resumes one.
commands.what: /resume opens All chats across workspaces by default (Left/Right switches to 'This workspace'). Chats from other directories show a folder label; resuming one loads the full conversation instead of an empty session.

whenGood.title: Keep working on a task started yesterday
whenGood.example: /resume — pick yesterday's matching session from the list

whenGood.title: Find a chat from a different repo again
whenGood.example: /resume — filter by folder label to find the right workspace session

whenBad.title: Only the most recently used session needs to continue
whenBad.why: /resume first shows a picker list, an unnecessary step when it's clear which session is meant.
whenBad.alternative: Use --continue at startup (shorthand for --resume=-1)

whenBad.title: You want a completely new session
whenBad.why: /resume continues existing chats but doesn't start anything new.
whenBad.alternative: Use /clear

commands.name: /fork
commands.summary: Branches the current chat into a new session, the original stays untouched.
commands.what: /fork branches the current conversation into a copy and lets you pursue a different path in it. Aliases per the changelog: /branch, /duplicate.

whenGood.title: Try two different solution approaches in parallel
whenGood.example: /fork — then test an alternative implementation path in the new chat

whenGood.title: Hedge a risky attempt without endangering the main session
whenGood.example: /fork — branch off before an experimental refactor

whenBad.title: The existing path should just continue
whenBad.why: A fork creates an extra session you then have to manage, even though only linear continuation is needed.
whenBad.alternative: Keep writing normally in the current chat

whenBad.title: Old failed attempts should disappear entirely
whenBad.why: /fork keeps the original around, it doesn't clean anything up.
whenBad.alternative: Use /rewind to jump back to an earlier state

commands.name: /summarize
commands.summary: Summarizes the conversation to free up space in the context window.
commands.what: /summarize compresses the conversation so far to free up context. The command was renamed from /compress (to match the IDE); /compact and /compress still work as aliases.

whenGood.title: A long session is about to blow past the context window
whenGood.example: /summarize — after many turns, before the agent loses track

whenGood.title: Clean up before a topic switch within the same session
whenGood.example: /summarize — keep the key points, drop the detail noise

whenBad.title: Important detail must not be lost
whenBad.why: Summarizing loses the exact wording and detail fidelity of earlier messages.
whenBad.alternative: Write down important facts explicitly beforehand, or use /fork to keep a copy with the full history

whenBad.title: The context is still small
whenBad.why: Summarizing a short session barely saves space but still costs nuance.
whenBad.alternative: Just keep working normally

commands.name: /rewind
commands.summary: Jumps back to an earlier message via an interactive timeline and restores files/conversation.
commands.what: /rewind opens an interactive timeline to reset files and conversation state to an earlier turn, with a per-turn diff and the option to restore only the conversation (without files). On by default. Aliases per the changelog: /undo, /restore (must first be enabled in /config). Rewinding to an earlier turn also restores that turn's image attachments.

whenGood.title: A failed approach needs to be fully undone
whenGood.example: /rewind — jump back to the turn before the failed refactor

whenGood.title: Reset only the conversation state, but keep the files
whenGood.example: /rewind — choose the 'conversation-only restore' option

whenBad.title: Only a single typo needs correcting
whenBad.why: Jumping back through the whole timeline is overkill for a small correction.
whenBad.alternative: Just give a new correction instruction directly in the chat

whenBad.title: Two solution paths should be kept in parallel
whenBad.why: /rewind overwrites the later state instead of keeping it alongside.
whenBad.alternative: Use /fork beforehand to keep a copy

commands.name: /vim
commands.summary: Toggles Vim keybindings in the input field.
commands.what: /vim toggles Vim keybindings in the prompt input field, including search motions and visual mode; editing can be triggered from any cursor position in the prompt, not just an empty input field.

whenGood.title: Use familiar Vim motions while writing long prompts
whenGood.example: /vim — then navigate the input field with hjkl/dw/ct.

whenBad.title: You're more comfortable with standard keybindings
whenBad.why: Vim keybindings change the behavior of familiar keys like i, dd, or Escape.
whenBad.alternative: Run /vim again to turn it off

commands.name: /line-numbers
commands.summary: Toggles line numbers in displayed code blocks.
commands.what: /line-numbers toggles whether line numbers are shown in the session's code blocks.

whenGood.title: Wanting to reference specific lines during review
whenGood.example: /line-numbers — turn on before saying 'check line 42'

whenBad.title: Just short code snippets with no line references
whenBad.why: For short snippets, line numbers get in the way more than they help.
whenBad.alternative: Leave /line-numbers off for a more compact view

commands.name: /show-thinking
commands.summary: Toggles display of the model's thinking blocks.
commands.what: /show-thinking toggles whether the model's internal thinking blocks are shown visibly in the session.

whenGood.title: Understand why the agent made a particular decision
whenGood.example: /show-thinking — turn on before a complex architecture decision

whenBad.title: Only the final result matters, not the reasoning path
whenBad.why: Thinking blocks unnecessarily lengthen the visible output when only the result matters.
whenBad.alternative: Leave /show-thinking off for a more compact session

commands.name: /status-indicators
commands.summary: Toggles status indicators in the terminal title.
commands.what: /status-indicators toggles whether the agent's status (e.g. waiting, working) is shown in the terminal window title.

whenGood.title: Spot the agent's status even with a minimized terminal window
whenGood.example: /status-indicators — on, while a long task runs in the background

whenBad.title: The terminal title is used for something else, e.g. a custom script
whenBad.why: The status indicator then overwrites your own title text.
whenBad.alternative: Leave /status-indicators off

commands.name: /shell
commands.summary: Switches to Shell mode to run short commands directly without leaving the conversation.
commands.what: /shell switches to Shell mode for short commands within the session. Aliases: /sh, /run.

whenGood.title: Quickly check git status without leaving the chat context
whenGood.example: /shell git status

whenGood.title: Briefly inspect a file before the agent keeps working
whenGood.example: /sh ls -la src/

whenBad.title: The command should be part of a multi-step task planned by the agent
whenBad.why: /shell runs the command in isolation, without folding it into the agent's task planning.
whenBad.alternative: Phrase the command as a normal prompt instead, so the agent runs it in context

whenBad.title: You need persistent network/write access with clear limits
whenBad.why: /shell follows the normal session policy but offers no explicit path/network limits.
whenBad.alternative: Use agent sandbox run with --allow-paths/--network

commands.name: /about
commands.summary: Shows CLI version, system, and account info and copies it to the clipboard.
commands.what: /about shows version, system, and account information and additionally copies it to the clipboard.

whenGood.title: Gather diagnostic info for a bug report
whenGood.example: /about — paste the output directly into a support ticket

whenBad.title: Only the bare version number is needed
whenBad.why: /about gives a whole info block, more than needed for a simple version check.
whenBad.alternative: Run agent --version in the terminal

commands.name: /setup-terminal
commands.summary: Configures terminal newline keybindings to match the detected terminal.
commands.what: /setup-terminal automatically sets up newline keybindings (e.g. Option+Enter) to match the detected terminal program.

whenGood.title: Comfortably write multi-line prompts
whenGood.example: /setup-terminal — run once after installation

whenBad.title: The terminal program doesn't support the reconfiguration
whenBad.why: Not every terminal allows automatically setting such keybindings.
whenBad.alternative: Configure newline keybindings manually in the terminal program

commands.name: /help
commands.summary: Shows help; with a command name as an argument, shows details for that command.
commands.what: /help shows an overview of available commands; /help <command> shows details for a specific command.

whenGood.title: Look up the syntax of a rarely used command
whenGood.example: /help mcp

whenBad.title: You already know exactly which command you need
whenBad.why: Going through help first just costs time then.
whenBad.alternative: Call the command directly

commands.name: /feedback
commands.summary: Sends feedback directly to the Cursor team.
commands.what: /feedback sends a message as feedback to the Cursor team.

whenGood.title: Report a bug or missing feature directly
whenGood.example: /feedback /rewind sometimes jumps back two turns instead of one

whenBad.title: It's an urgent, individual support request
whenBad.why: Feedback messages aren't built for fast 1:1 support.
whenBad.alternative: Use Cursor's official support channel

commands.name: /open
commands.summary: Opens the current repo's Git root directory in the Cursor editor.
commands.what: /open opens the repository's Git root directory in Cursor. Alias: /cursor.

whenGood.title: Continue visually in the editor after CLI work
whenGood.example: /open — after the agent has proposed changes

whenBad.title: Cursor (the editor) isn't installed or wanted at all
whenBad.why: /open assumes a local Cursor installation.
whenBad.alternative: Review changes directly in the terminal with /changes instead

commands.name: /copy-request-id
commands.summary: Copies the last request's ID to the clipboard.
commands.what: /copy-request-id copies the request ID of the last request to the clipboard, useful for support and debugging.

whenGood.title: Report a specific problem to support
whenGood.example: /copy-request-id — paste the ID directly into a support ticket

whenBad.title: It's about the whole conversation, not a single request
whenBad.why: The request ID only refers to the last individual request.
whenBad.alternative: Use /copy-conversation-id

commands.name: /copy-conversation-id
commands.summary: Copies the current conversation's ID to the clipboard.
commands.what: /copy-conversation-id copies the current session's conversation ID to the clipboard.

whenGood.title: Find or reference a session later by its ID
whenGood.example: /copy-conversation-id — save the ID in a note

whenBad.title: It's about a single request, not the whole session
whenBad.why: The conversation ID identifies the entire session, not a single request.
whenBad.alternative: Use /copy-request-id

commands.name: /logout
commands.summary: Signs out of the current Cursor account.
commands.what: /logout signs the CLI out of the currently signed-in Cursor account.

whenGood.title: Remove the account on a shared machine
whenGood.example: /logout — before switching to a different Cursor account

whenBad.title: You just want to end the session briefly, not sign out of the account
whenBad.why: /logout clears the login, requiring a fresh login flow afterward.
whenBad.alternative: Use /quit or /exit to just end the session

commands.name: /quit
commands.summary: Exits the current CLI session.
commands.what: /quit exits the CLI session.

whenGood.title: Work is done for this session
whenGood.example: /quit — after all changes have been applied

whenBad.title: You want to continue this exact session later
whenBad.why: After /quit the session first has to be found again via /resume.
whenBad.alternative: Use /rename beforehand to make the session easier to find later

commands.name: /exit
commands.summary: Exits the current CLI session, identical to /quit.
commands.what: /exit exits the CLI session, functionally identical to /quit.

whenGood.title: Work is done for this session
whenGood.example: /exit — after all changes have been applied

whenBad.title: Mixing both commands out of habit
whenBad.why: /exit and /quit do exactly the same thing, one of them is enough.
whenBad.alternative: Use one of the two consistently

commands.name: /mcp
commands.summary: Manages MCP servers via an interactive menu: log in, list, view tools.
commands.what: /mcp opens a menu to manage MCP servers: filter, log in, list a server's tools, view server details. Servers are grouped by scope (User/Project/Team), and failed servers show their actual error.

whenGood.title: Check a new MCP server's tools before using it
whenGood.example: /mcp list-tools github

whenGood.title: Do an OAuth login for a team MCP server
whenGood.example: /mcp — select the server, then 'Login'

whenBad.title: All MCP servers should be auto-approved at startup
whenBad.why: /mcp is meant for interactive management, not automated approval in scripts.
whenBad.alternative: Use --approve-mcps at startup

whenBad.title: A server should disappear from the config permanently
whenBad.why: /mcp only manages connection status and approvals at runtime.
whenBad.alternative: Remove the server entry directly from mcp.json

commands.name: /plugin
commands.summary: Manages plugins and plugin marketplaces.
commands.what: /plugin manages plugins and marketplaces: add a marketplace by git URL (/plugin marketplace add), browse, install, and uninstall plugins by scope. Plugin skills, slash commands, subagents, and MCP servers all load into the session.

whenGood.title: Add a team marketplace
whenGood.example: /plugin marketplace add https://github.com/team/cursor-plugins

whenGood.title: Install a plugin for just this one project
whenGood.example: /plugin — select the plugin, set scope to 'Project'

whenBad.title: Just testing a locally sitting plugin folder, no marketplace
whenBad.why: Going through a marketplace entry for that isn't necessary.
whenBad.alternative: Use --plugin-dir <path> at startup

whenBad.title: Plugins should be centrally mandated for the whole team
whenBad.why: /plugin is local, per-user interactive management.
whenBad.alternative: Set team-wide plugin configuration via Cursor's admin settings

commands.name: /config
commands.summary: Opens an interactive editor for CLI settings.
commands.what: /config opens a full settings editor inside the CLI, including a version and account page, instead of hand-editing cli-config.json. It can, for example, toggle model shortcuts or Rewind on/off.

whenGood.title: Adjust several settings in one place
whenGood.example: /config — e.g. set the sandbox default and the status line in one pass

whenGood.title: Look up an unclear setting without knowing the JSON structure
whenGood.example: /config — browse the categories instead of searching cli-config.json

whenBad.title: The same setting needs to be rolled out to many machines automatically
whenBad.why: Interactive per-session editing doesn't scale for rollouts.
whenBad.alternative: Edit and distribute cli-config.json directly in a scriptable way

whenBad.title: Only sandbox options need adjusting
whenBad.why: /config shows all categories, more than needed for a single sandbox setting.
whenBad.alternative: Use /sandbox directly

commands.name: /copy
commands.summary: Copies an earlier own message to the clipboard.
commands.what: /copy copies an earlier message you sent in the session to the clipboard.

whenGood.title: Reuse a previously used prompt in a different session
whenGood.example: /copy — select the matching message, then paste it into a new chat

whenBad.title: The agent's full response should be copied, not your own message
whenBad.why: /copy applies to your own messages, not agent responses.
whenBad.alternative: Manually select and copy the agent response in the terminal

commands.name: /sandbox
commands.summary: Configures sandbox mode and network access via an interactive menu.
commands.what: /sandbox opens a menu to configure sandbox mode and network access settings. Sandbox policy files (~/.cursor/sandbox.json, .cursor/sandbox.json) are honored, including over SSH; a default-deny network proxy applies to sandboxed commands.

whenGood.title: Restrict write access while trusting an unfamiliar repo
whenGood.example: /sandbox — enable before the agent works on a foreign project

whenGood.title: Allow network access specifically for one task
whenGood.example: /sandbox — adjust the network mode before a package install

whenBad.title: A single command should run isolated with its own path rules, not the whole session
whenBad.why: /sandbox sets the policy for the running session, not for a one-off command.
whenBad.alternative: Use agent sandbox run --allow-paths <paths> for a single command

whenBad.title: Everything should just run without any prompts at all
whenBad.why: Sandbox deliberately restricts, which conflicts with a fully autonomous run.
whenBad.alternative: Use --yolo or /run-everything

commands.name: /bedrock
commands.summary: Configures AWS Bedrock as the model backend, if the Bedrock feature is enabled.
commands.what: /bedrock configures AWS Bedrock as the model backend, either with your own AWS credentials or a team IAM role. Only works if the Bedrock feature is enabled for the account.

whenGood.title: Set up Bedrock as a backend with your own AWS keys
whenGood.example: /bedrock — enter your own credentials

whenBad.title: The account doesn't use AWS Bedrock at all
whenBad.why: Without the Bedrock feature enabled, the command has no effect.
whenBad.alternative: Choose a model normally via /model

commands.name: /add-dir
commands.summary: Adds another directory to a multi-root session at runtime.
commands.what: /add-dir adds an extra directory to the running session to work across multiple repos at once. Slash skills and custom commands from the new directory refresh immediately; a restart is only needed for the agent to auto-discover new skills. Documented in the official changelog, not (yet) listed in the command reference table.

whenGood.title: Pull in a second, related repo to the running session
whenGood.example: /add-dir ../shared-lib — to coordinate changes across both repos at once

whenGood.title: Reuse the directory combination later
whenGood.example: /add-dir ../shared-lib — then save it as a set with /save-workspace

whenBad.title: The multi-root combination is already known before starting
whenBad.why: Adding it later mid-session is then an unnecessary extra step.
whenBad.alternative: Use --add-dir <path> at startup (repeatable, combinable with --workspace)

whenBad.title: Working on just a single directory
whenBad.why: An extra root directory grows the context the agent has to track.
whenBad.alternative: Skip /add-dir and stick to one workspace

commands.name: /save-workspace
commands.summary: Saves the currently active multi-root directories as a named workspace set.
commands.what: /save-workspace saves the set of directories currently added via /add-dir under a name, so it can be reloaded later. Documented in the official changelog, not (yet) listed in the command reference table.

whenGood.title: Name a recurring repo combination
whenGood.example: /save-workspace backend-plus-shared-lib

whenBad.title: The directory combination is only needed once
whenBad.why: Saving it as a named set isn't worth it for a one-off session.
whenBad.alternative: Just use /add-dir without /save-workspace

commands.name: /load-workspace
commands.summary: Loads a multi-root workspace set previously saved with /save-workspace.
commands.what: /load-workspace loads a previously saved combination of directories back into the session. Documented in the official changelog, not (yet) listed in the command reference table.

whenGood.title: Quickly return to the usual multi-repo setup
whenGood.example: /load-workspace backend-plus-shared-lib

whenBad.title: No saved workspace set exists yet
whenBad.why: /load-workspace requires a prior /save-workspace call.
whenBad.alternative: Add directories manually with /add-dir

commands.name: /auto-review
commands.summary: Toggles 'Auto-review' run mode, a middle ground between allowlist and Run Everything.
commands.what: /auto-review turns on Auto-review mode. A classifier checks shell, MCP, and fetch calls in order: allowlisted calls run immediately, sandboxable calls run in the sandbox, and the rest go to the classifier, which allows the call, tries a different approach, or asks for approval. Also controllable via --auto-review or in /config; allow/block rules in permissions.json steer the classifier. Existing hard approval boundaries are unchanged. Documented in the official changelog, not (yet) listed in the command reference table.

whenGood.title: Fewer prompts, without going all the way to 'Run Everything'
whenGood.example: /auto-review — for a long session with a mix of harmless and sensitive commands

whenGood.title: Use fixed allow/block rules per team
whenGood.example: /auto-review — combined with matching entries in permissions.json

whenBad.title: Full manual control over every single command is wanted
whenBad.why: Auto-review deliberately reduces the number of prompts.
whenBad.alternative: Leave Auto-review off, stay in the default allowlist mode

whenBad.title: You want truly zero prompts
whenBad.why: Auto-review still asks in unclear cases.
whenBad.alternative: Use /run-everything

commands.name: /context
commands.summary: Visualizes what's consuming the context window, broken down by category.
commands.what: /context shows a breakdown of what's consuming the current session's context window, e.g. by category such as files, tool output, or conversation. Documented in the official changelog, not (yet) listed in the command reference table.

whenGood.title: Understand what's taking up the most space before a /summarize
whenGood.example: /context — check whether, e.g., large tool outputs are filling the context

whenGood.title: Gauge how much room is left for further turns
whenGood.example: /context — before a planned large file review

whenBad.title: The context window needs to actually shrink, not just be analyzed
whenBad.why: /context only shows the breakdown, it doesn't change anything.
whenBad.alternative: Use /summarize to actually free up space

whenBad.title: It's just a quick yes/no check mid-turn
whenBad.why: /context opens its own detail view, more than needed for a brief aside.
whenBad.alternative: Use /btw for a quick read-only side question

commands.name: /full-conversation
commands.summary: Turns off the performance-optimized short rendering and shows the full conversation history.
commands.what: Long conversations are, by default, only partially redrawn (only recent turns), keeping resizes and resumes fast. /full-conversation turns this off and shows the full history again. Documented in the official changelog, not (yet) listed in the command reference table.

whenGood.title: Scroll through the full conversation history of a very long session
whenGood.example: /full-conversation — on, to see older turns again

whenBad.title: Only working on recent turns, performance matters more
whenBad.why: The full history makes resizes and resumes noticeably slower in long sessions.
whenBad.alternative: Leave /full-conversation off (default)

commands.name: /btw
commands.summary: Asks a quick, read-only side question mid-turn, without adding to the chat history.
commands.what: /btw asks a side question mid-turn; the answer streams into its own dismissible overlay with full conversation context and never lands in the actual chat history. Documented in the official changelog, not (yet) listed in the command reference table.

whenGood.title: Quickly clarify an understanding question without interrupting the running task
whenGood.example: /btw Which file did you just change last?

whenGood.title: Ask a detail question without filling the main history with side matter
whenGood.example: /btw Why was this library chosen over another one?

whenBad.title: The answer should permanently be part of the conversation
whenBad.why: /btw answers land in the overlay, not the chat history, and are lost as context for later turns.
whenBad.alternative: Ask the question as a normal chat message instead

whenBad.title: The question should actually change files
whenBad.why: /btw is explicitly read-only.
whenBad.alternative: Use a normal message in agent mode

commands.name: /skills
commands.summary: Browses available skills; individual skills can also be invoked directly via /skill-name.
commands.what: /skills provides a browsing menu for available skills, including curated built-in skills. Individual skills can also be invoked inline directly via /skill-name. Skills are discovered in, among others, .cursor/skills as well as .claude/skills, .agents/skills, and .codex/skills, including nested in subdirectories. Documented in the official changelog, not (yet) listed in the command reference table.

whenGood.title: Discover available skills in an unfamiliar project
whenGood.example: /skills — browse which skills are defined in the repo

whenGood.title: Invoke a known skill directly
whenGood.example: /skill-name — directly instead of via the browsing menu

whenBad.title: A skill should be available to the model but not show up in autocomplete
whenBad.why: /skills and /skill-name show all user-invocable skills.
whenBad.alternative: Set user-invocable: false in the skill's SKILL.md frontmatter

whenBad.title: It's about your own custom commands, not skills
whenBad.why: /skills shows skills, not self-defined custom commands.
whenBad.alternative: Use /commands

commands.name: /rules
commands.summary: Interactive browser for viewing and editing the loaded Cursor rules.
commands.what: /rules opens an interactive browser for the currently loaded Cursor rules, including inline editing. Rules in .cursor/rules in subdirectories are discovered everywhere, matching the IDE. Documented in the official changelog, not (yet) listed in the command reference table.

whenGood.title: Check which rules are active in the current session
whenGood.example: /rules — check which project conventions apply before a task

whenBad.title: It's about skills or custom commands, not rules
whenBad.why: /rules only shows Cursor rules.
whenBad.alternative: Use /skills or /commands

commands.name: /commands
commands.summary: Interactive browser for viewing and editing your own custom commands.
commands.what: /commands opens an interactive browser for self-defined custom commands, including inline editing. Documented in the official changelog, not (yet) listed in the command reference table.

whenGood.title: Look up an existing custom command before using it
whenGood.example: /commands — browse which custom commands are defined in the project

whenBad.title: It's about built-in skills, not self-defined commands
whenBad.why: /commands only shows your own custom commands.
whenBad.alternative: Use /skills

commands.name: /changes
commands.summary: Opens the unified review UI for proposed file changes (Ctrl+R).
commands.what: /changes (keybinding Ctrl+R) opens the review UI for proposed changes. The 'Session' tab shows only this session's edits; o/O opens diffs in your editor, and there's a zen toggle for reviewing. Documented in the official changelog, not (yet) listed in the command reference table.

whenGood.title: Review all proposed changes together before committing
whenGood.example: /changes — go through the Session tab before the agent finalizes the files

whenGood.title: Look closer at a single diff in your usual editor
whenGood.example: /changes — select a file, then press o to open it in the editor

whenBad.title: Just briefly confirming a single, already-known change
whenBad.why: The full review UI is more overhead than needed for a simple single confirmation.
whenBad.alternative: Just confirm the direct prompt in the chat normally

whenBad.title: Changes from earlier sessions should be reviewed too
whenBad.why: The Session tab deliberately shows only the current session.
whenBad.alternative: Check git diff or the full git history in the terminal

commands.name: /opus, /composer, /fast
commands.summary: Model shortcuts: jump straight to a model family or toggle Fast mode, without opening the model picker.
commands.what: /opus and /composer jump straight to the respective model family, each shortcut remembering its last-chosen variant; /fast toggles Fast mode when the current model supports it. Can be disabled under 'Model slash commands' in /config. Documented in the official changelog, not (yet) listed in the command reference table.

whenGood.title: Quickly switch to a known model without opening the picker
whenGood.example: /opus

whenBad.title: An unfamiliar or first-time model needs to be searched for
whenBad.why: The shortcuts jump straight to a fixed family, with no search across other models.
whenBad.alternative: Use /model [filter] for the fuzzy model search

commands.name: agent [prompt...]
commands.summary: Starts the Cursor CLI in default agent mode, optionally with a prompt passed directly as an argument.
commands.what: Running agent with no subcommand starts an interactive session in agent mode – the default mode with full tool access (read, write, shell commands, web). An optional prompt argument is sent as the first message right away, e.g. agent "refactor the auth module to use JWT tokens". Without an argument it opens an empty chat window. For non-interactive runs (scripts, CI), the -p/--print flag additionally makes it print only the final response.

whenGood.title: Start directly with a concrete task instead of typing it in afterwards
whenGood.example: agent "fix the failing tests in src/auth"

whenGood.title: Start an interactive session for exploratory work with no fixed task yet
whenGood.example: agent — then clarify goals and questions step by step in the chat

whenBad.title: The run needs to happen unattended in a script or CI job
whenBad.why: Default agent mode interactively asks for approval before running commands, which blocks unattended runs.
whenBad.alternative: Combine -p with --force, e.g. agent -p --force "<prompt>" (see the collective Global Options entry)

whenBad.title: Only reading/analysis is wanted, no file changes
whenBad.why: Default mode has full write and shell access.
whenBad.alternative: Set --mode ask at startup, or use /ask after starting

commands.name: agent login
commands.summary: Authenticates the CLI with your Cursor account through the browser login flow.
commands.what: agent login opens the browser login flow and links the CLI to your Cursor account. Authentication without a browser is also possible via --api-key (or the CURSOR_API_KEY environment variable) or --auth-token (CURSOR_AUTH_TOKEN). Setting NO_OPEN_BROWSER=1 makes it print the login URL instead of opening a browser automatically, useful on remote servers without a GUI.

whenGood.title: First-time CLI setup on a new machine
whenGood.example: agent login

whenBad.title: The CLI runs headless on a server with no browser access
whenBad.why: agent login normally requires an interactive browser flow.
whenBad.alternative: Set auth via --api-key/CURSOR_API_KEY, or export NO_OPEN_BROWSER=1 so only the login URL is printed

commands.name: agent logout
commands.summary: Signs the CLI out of your Cursor account and clears locally stored credentials.
commands.what: agent logout signs the current CLI installation out and removes the locally stored credentials (by default from the macOS keychain; with AGENT_CLI_CREDENTIAL_STORE=file from an unencrypted file instead).

whenGood.title: Switching accounts on a shared machine
whenGood.example: agent logout — then agent login with the other account

whenBad.title: You only want to check the auth status, not sign out
whenBad.why: agent logout deletes the stored credentials immediately.
whenBad.alternative: Use agent status or agent whoami to just view the status

commands.name: agent status | agent whoami
commands.summary: Shows the current authentication status plus account and endpoint info.
commands.what: agent status (alias: agent whoami) shows whether and with which account the CLI is signed in, including the endpoint configuration. --format json returns the output as machine-readable JSON instead of the default text format.

whenGood.title: Check whether the CLI is signed in before a script run
whenGood.example: agent status --format json

whenBad.title: You want to switch accounts, not just look one up
whenBad.why: agent status changes nothing, it only shows the current state.
whenBad.alternative: Use agent logout followed by agent login

commands.name: agent about
commands.summary: Shows version, system, and account info for the CLI installation.
commands.what: agent about prints an overview of the installed CLI version, the system, and the signed-in account. --format json makes the output machine-readable. /about does the same in interactive mode and also copies the info to the clipboard.

whenGood.title: Gather diagnostic info for a support request
whenGood.example: agent about --format json

whenBad.title: Only the version number is needed
whenBad.why: agent about returns more info than a plain version check needs.
whenBad.alternative: Use agent -v or agent --version instead (see Global Options)

commands.name: agent models
commands.summary: Lists all models available for the account.
commands.what: agent models shows the list of models usable by the currently signed-in account. This is the same result as agent --list-models. A model is then selected separately, either with --model <model> at startup or via /model in a running session.

whenGood.title: Check the exact model name to use before a script run
whenGood.example: agent models

whenGood.title: Pipe the available models into another tool or script
whenGood.example: agent --list-models > models.txt

whenBad.title: You just want to switch the model for the current session
whenBad.why: agent models only lists models, it doesn't switch anything.
whenBad.alternative: Use /model in the running chat, or set --model <model> at startup

whenBad.title: You're already in an interactive session and want a quick switch
whenBad.why: A new agent models call would mean a new session or process.
whenBad.alternative: Use /model directly inside the running session

commands.name: agent mcp
commands.summary: Parent command for managing configured MCP servers (login, list, tools, enable/disable).
commands.what: agent mcp groups all subcommands for managing Model Context Protocol servers: login, list, list-tools, enable, disable. The CLI uses the same mcp.json configuration as the Cursor editor (project and global level) and picks up servers from parent directories automatically. agent mcp list (or /mcp list in interactive mode) opens an interactive selection menu.

whenGood.title: Get an overview of which MCP servers are configured at all
whenGood.example: agent mcp list

whenGood.title: Skip all MCP approval prompts during a scripted run
whenGood.example: agent --approve-mcps -p "query my database for recent errors"

whenBad.title: An MCP tool should be used directly, not just managed
whenBad.why: agent mcp only manages server configuration, it doesn't call tools itself.
whenBad.alternative: Send a normal prompt to agent or agent -p — MCP tools are picked up automatically when relevant

whenBad.title: A new MCP server needs to be set up for the first time
whenBad.why: agent mcp only manages servers already entered in mcp.json.
whenBad.alternative: First add the server to .cursor/mcp.json or ~/.cursor/mcp.json, then approve it with agent mcp enable

commands.name: agent sandbox
commands.summary: Parent command, hidden from default help, for configuring sandbox mode or running one command in isolation.
commands.what: agent sandbox groups the enable, disable, reset, and run subcommands, which configure the CLI's sandbox mode or run a single command in isolation. Per the docs, agent sandbox is 'hidden' as a top-level command and therefore doesn't show up in default help (agent help). The same configuration can also be controlled interactively via /sandbox or the --sandbox <mode> flag.

whenGood.title: Turn on sandbox mode globally for all future sessions
whenGood.example: agent sandbox enable

whenGood.title: Test a single, potentially risky command in isolation
whenGood.example: agent sandbox run npm install

whenBad.title: Sandbox mode should only apply to a single session
whenBad.why: agent sandbox enable/disable changes a persistent setting that carries over across sessions.
whenBad.alternative: Set --sandbox enabled or --sandbox disabled only for that one agent call

whenBad.title: You just want to check which sandbox mode is currently active
whenBad.why: agent sandbox with no subcommand opens a configuration menu directly instead of just displaying status.
whenBad.alternative: Open /sandbox in interactive mode and view the current state there without changing anything

commands.name: agent worker
commands.summary: Parent command for starting a private cloud worker that runs agent tasks in your own environment.
commands.what: agent worker connects your own machine to Cursor and lets cloud-agent tasks run there instead of in Cursor's own cloud environment (self-hosted). Contains the start and debug subcommands. The worker is configured via flags like --worker-dir, --name, --label or --labels-file, --pool or --pool-name, and --idle-release-timeout.

whenGood.title: Provide your own hardware/environment for cloud-agent runs (privacy, special tooling)
whenGood.example: agent worker start --name build-worker-1 --worker-dir ~/repos/myapp

whenGood.title: Register several workers as a pool that cloud agents can draw from
whenGood.example: agent worker start --pool --pool-name ci-pool

whenBad.title: Only a single local agent run is needed
whenBad.why: agent worker starts a long-running process connected to Cursor for cloud-agent handoffs, not a one-off run.
whenBad.alternative: Use a normal interactive session with agent or agent -p

whenBad.title: Configuration/connectivity should be checked before actually starting
whenBad.why: agent worker start connects immediately; auth, privacy, or routing problems only surface afterwards.
whenBad.alternative: Run agent worker debug first to see preflight diagnostics

commands.name: agent acp
commands.summary: Starts the CLI in ACP server mode (Agent Client Protocol) for editor and client integrations.
commands.what: agent acp starts Cursor Agent as an ACP server over stdio, using newline-delimited JSON-RPC 2.0 messages, e.g. for editor integrations like Zed or JetBrains. Per the docs the command is 'hidden' and meant for building custom clients, not normal terminal use. Authentication happens beforehand via agent login, --api-key/CURSOR_API_KEY, or --auth-token/CURSOR_AUTH_TOKEN; endpoint and TLS options (-e, -k) are appended before acp, e.g. agent -e https://api2.cursor.sh acp.

whenGood.title: Build a custom client or editor integration against Cursor Agent
whenGood.example: agent acp — then send initialize/session/new/session/prompt via JSON-RPC over stdin/stdout

whenGood.title: Authenticate up front with an API key instead of logging in through the ACP flow
whenGood.example: agent --api-key "$CURSOR_API_KEY" acp

whenBad.title: Normal terminal use without building a client
whenBad.why: agent acp only outputs raw JSON-RPC messages on stdout, not a readable chat UI.
whenBad.alternative: Just use agent (interactive mode) or agent -p for scripts

whenBad.title: External tools/data need to be made available to the agent
whenBad.why: ACP is for client integrations, not for wiring tools into the agent.
whenBad.alternative: Use agent mcp to connect MCP servers for tool access

commands.name: agent update
commands.summary: Updates Cursor Agent to the latest version.
commands.what: agent update downloads and installs the latest CLI version. Background auto-updates are enabled by default; /update does the same thing directly from a running interactive session.

whenGood.title: Manually update to the latest version, e.g. to use a new feature flag
whenGood.example: agent update

whenBad.title: Automatic background updates are unwanted (e.g. a pinned version in CI)
whenBad.why: agent update is a manual one-off command and doesn't affect auto-update behavior.
whenBad.alternative: Set --disable-auto-update to turn off background updates entirely (see Global Options)

commands.name: agent ls
commands.summary: Opens a list of previous chats to pick one to resume.
commands.what: agent ls shows previous chat sessions to choose from, similar to /resume in interactive mode. The name is a bit misleading: despite 'ls' it doesn't list files, it lists sessions.

whenGood.title: You're unsure which of the recent sessions to resume
whenGood.example: agent ls

whenBad.title: You already know it should be the most recently used session
whenBad.why: agent ls shows a selection list first, adding an extra step.
whenBad.alternative: Use agent resume or agent --continue to resume the last session directly

commands.name: agent resume
commands.summary: Resumes the most recently used chat session directly, without showing a selection list.
commands.what: agent resume loads the most recently used session, including its prior context, and continues the conversation there. It's equivalent to --continue or the /resume slash command, both of which are shorthand for --resume=-1.

whenGood.title: Pick up the last conversation directly after an interruption
whenGood.example: agent resume

whenBad.title: An older session, not the most recent one, needs to be resumed
whenBad.why: agent resume always loads only the most recently used session.
whenBad.alternative: Use agent ls to pick a specific earlier session, or set --resume [chatId] with a concrete ID

commands.name: agent create-chat
commands.summary: Creates a new, empty chat and returns its ID, e.g. for use in scripts.
commands.what: agent create-chat creates an empty chat session and prints its ID to the console, without starting an interactive session directly. The ID can then be targeted again with --resume [chatId], for example from a script.

whenGood.title: Reserve a session ID up front to address it later from a script via --resume
whenGood.example: CHAT_ID=$(agent create-chat); agent --resume "$CHAT_ID" -p "start task"

whenBad.title: You just want to jump in and start chatting with the agent right away
whenBad.why: agent create-chat only creates an empty session, it doesn't start a conversation.
whenBad.alternative: Just use agent or agent "<prompt>"

commands.name: agent generate-rule | agent rule
commands.summary: Walks through interactive prompts to generate a new Cursor rule.
commands.what: agent generate-rule (alias: agent rule) walks you through creating a new rule interactively, which then lands in the CLI's rules system (.cursor/rules). The CLI automatically loads rules from .cursor/rules and applies them at startup; AGENTS.md and CLAUDE.md at the project root are also read as rules.

whenGood.title: Capture recurring project behavior as a persistent rule
whenGood.example: agent generate-rule — e.g. create a rule for the project's preferred test runner

whenGood.title: Make team-wide conventions available consistently across all sessions
whenGood.example: agent rule — generate a rule about commit message format or branch naming

whenBad.title: The instruction only applies to the current session, not permanently
whenBad.why: A generated rule lands in .cursor/rules and applies project-wide to future sessions.
whenBad.alternative: Just state the instruction directly in the prompt instead of creating a rule

whenBad.title: An existing rule needs editing, not a new one
whenBad.why: agent generate-rule always creates a new rule via the interactive flow.
whenBad.alternative: Edit the file in .cursor/rules directly, or use /rules in interactive mode to browse and edit

commands.name: agent install-shell-integration
commands.summary: Installs the CLI's shell integration into ~/.zshrc.
commands.what: agent install-shell-integration sets up the shell integration in ~/.zshrc. Per the docs this explicitly targets zsh; no separate install routine is documented for other shells.

whenGood.title: Set up shell integration once after installing the CLI
whenGood.example: agent install-shell-integration

whenBad.title: Shell integration needs to be removed again
whenBad.why: agent install-shell-integration only installs, it doesn't remove anything.
whenBad.alternative: Use agent uninstall-shell-integration

commands.name: agent uninstall-shell-integration
commands.summary: Removes the CLI's shell integration from ~/.zshrc again.
commands.what: agent uninstall-shell-integration reverses what agent install-shell-integration set up and removes the corresponding entries from ~/.zshrc.

whenGood.title: Cleanly reset shell integration when it's causing problems
whenGood.example: agent uninstall-shell-integration

whenBad.title: Shell integration should only be paused briefly, not removed
whenBad.why: agent uninstall-shell-integration deletes the configuration entirely instead of just disabling it.
whenBad.alternative: Manually comment out the relevant block in ~/.zshrc

commands.name: agent help [command]
commands.summary: Shows help for the CLI or for a specific command.
commands.what: agent help [command] shows general help, or, with a command name as argument, detailed help for exactly that command. It's functionally equivalent to the global -h/--help flag, which can be used on any individual command.

whenGood.title: Look up the available options of a subcommand
whenGood.example: agent help worker

whenBad.title: You just want to quickly see the options of the command you're already typing
whenBad.why: agent help command means a separate call instead of appending it right to the command.
whenBad.alternative: Append -h or --help directly to the command, e.g. agent worker --help

commands.name: agent mcp login <identifier>
commands.summary: Authenticates with an MCP server configured in mcp.json.
commands.what: agent mcp login <identifier> starts a login flow with automatic callback handling for an MCP server configured in .cursor/mcp.json or ~/.cursor/mcp.json. Once login succeeds, the agent immediately has access to the server's authenticated tools.

whenGood.title: Make an MCP server with its own authentication (e.g. OAuth) usable for the first time
whenGood.example: agent mcp login linear

whenBad.title: The server is already configured but just needs to be approved locally
whenBad.why: agent mcp login handles authentication, not approving the server.
whenBad.alternative: Use agent mcp enable <identifier>

commands.name: agent mcp list
commands.summary: Lists configured MCP servers and their status (connected/disconnected, source, transport).
commands.what: agent mcp list opens an interactive menu with all configured MCP servers: name, connection status, configuration source (project or global), and transport method (stdio, HTTP, SSE). In interactive mode /mcp list gives the same overview.

whenGood.title: Check which MCP servers are currently connected before a task
whenGood.example: agent mcp list

whenBad.title: You want to see the parameters of a specific tool, not just the server list
whenBad.why: agent mcp list only shows server status, no tool details.
whenBad.alternative: Use agent mcp list-tools <identifier> for a specific server

commands.name: agent mcp list-tools <identifier>
commands.summary: Lists the available tools and their parameters for a specific MCP server.
commands.what: agent mcp list-tools <identifier> shows tool names, descriptions, and required/optional parameters with their types for a single MCP server.

whenGood.title: Check which parameters an MCP tool expects before using it
whenGood.example: agent mcp list-tools playwright

whenBad.title: You don't know the server identifier yet
whenBad.why: agent mcp list-tools requires a concrete identifier as an argument.
whenBad.alternative: Use agent mcp list first to see the configured identifiers

commands.name: agent mcp enable <identifier>
commands.summary: Adds an MCP server to the locally approved list.
commands.what: agent mcp enable <identifier> puts a configured MCP server on the locally approved list, so it can be used without a repeated approval prompt.

whenGood.title: Permanently approve a server you already trust
whenGood.example: agent mcp enable github

whenBad.title: All configured servers should be approved at once for a single script run
whenBad.why: agent mcp enable acts per server and permanently, not for a single run.
whenBad.alternative: Use the --approve-mcps flag on that particular agent call

commands.name: agent mcp disable <identifier>
commands.summary: Disables an MCP server so it stops loading or prompting for approval.
commands.what: agent mcp disable <identifier> fully disables a configured MCP server; it will neither load nor prompt for approval going forward.

whenGood.title: Turn off a broken or unneeded MCP server
whenGood.example: agent mcp disable old-server

whenBad.title: The server should only be ignored briefly for one run
whenBad.why: agent mcp disable is a persistent, server-scoped setting.
whenBad.alternative: Simply don't invoke that server in the relevant prompt, or adjust a project-specific mcp.json

commands.name: agent sandbox enable
commands.summary: Enables sandbox mode for command execution.
commands.what: agent sandbox enable turns sandbox mode on: commands then run with read/write access to the workspace but isolated from the rest of the system (network disabled by default). The setting persists across sessions.

whenGood.title: Turn on persistently isolated command execution for all future sessions
whenGood.example: agent sandbox enable

whenBad.title: The sandbox should only apply to a single command, not permanently
whenBad.why: agent sandbox enable changes the persistent configuration for all future runs.
whenBad.alternative: Use agent sandbox run <cmd> to run just one command in isolation

commands.name: agent sandbox disable
commands.summary: Disables sandbox mode and falls back to allowlist mode instead.
commands.what: agent sandbox disable turns sandbox mode off. The CLI then falls back to allowlist mode, where individual commands must be approved manually instead of running isolated automatically.

whenGood.title: Sandbox restrictions (e.g. no network) permanently get in the way of a particular workflow
whenGood.example: agent sandbox disable

whenBad.title: Network access is only needed for a single sandboxed run
whenBad.why: agent sandbox disable turns off the sandbox entirely instead of just allowing network.
whenBad.alternative: Use agent sandbox run <cmd> --network to allow network access just for that run

commands.name: agent sandbox reset
commands.summary: Resets the sandbox configuration to its default values.
commands.what: agent sandbox reset discards custom sandbox configuration (e.g. allow/readonly paths, network setting) and restores the default values.

whenGood.title: Restore a clean starting point after experimenting with paths/rules
whenGood.example: agent sandbox reset

whenBad.title: Only a single setting needs to change, not all of them
whenBad.why: agent sandbox reset resets the entire configuration, not just one value.
whenBad.alternative: Use agent sandbox enable/disable or the --allow-paths/--network flags on sandbox run in a targeted way

commands.name: agent sandbox run <cmd> [args...]
commands.summary: Runs a single command in a sandbox with read/write access to the workspace.
commands.what: agent sandbox run <cmd> [args...] runs exactly one command in isolation, by default with read/write access to the workspace (--sandbox, default true) and no network access (--network, default false). Extra paths can be exposed via --allow-paths or --readonly-paths, and unwanted files blocked via --blocked-patterns (gitignore syntax); --sb-debug writes debug logs to a temp folder.

whenGood.title: Test an unfamiliar install/build script in isolation before it touches the real workspace
whenGood.example: agent sandbox run npm install

whenGood.title: Run a command with network access even though the sandbox otherwise blocks it
whenGood.example: agent sandbox run --network curl https://example.com/healthz

whenBad.title: Several commands need to run isolated persistently, not just one
whenBad.why: agent sandbox run is meant for exactly one single command.
whenBad.alternative: Use agent sandbox enable so the sandbox policy applies to all future commands the CLI runs

whenBad.title: The command needs access outside the workspace directory
whenBad.why: Without extra flags, access is limited to the workspace.
whenBad.alternative: Specify --allow-paths or --readonly-paths with the additionally needed paths

commands.name: agent sandbox help [command]
commands.summary: Shows help for the sandbox subcommands.
commands.what: agent sandbox help [command] shows help for the sandbox subcommands (enable, disable, reset, run), optionally in detail for a single subcommand.

whenGood.title: Look up the available options of sandbox run
whenGood.example: agent sandbox help run

whenBad.title: You just want to quickly see the options of the sandbox command you're already typing
whenBad.why: agent sandbox help command is a separate call.
whenBad.alternative: Append -h or --help directly, e.g. agent sandbox run --help

commands.name: agent worker start
commands.summary: Starts the private worker and connects it to Cursor.
commands.what: agent worker start starts the private worker process and connects it to Cursor, so cloud-agent tasks run in your own environment instead of Cursor's cloud. It's configured via flags like --worker-dir (workspace root, repeatable, the first value sets the assignment identity), --name, --label/--labels-file, --pool/--pool-name, --idle-release-timeout, and --management-addr for /healthz, /readyz, /metrics. --verbose enables verbose startup logs.

whenGood.title: Expose several repos at once as worker workspaces
whenGood.example: agent worker start --worker-dir ~/repos/app-a --worker-dir ~/repos/app-b

whenGood.title: Expose health/metrics endpoints for monitoring the worker
whenGood.example: agent worker start --management-addr 0.0.0.0:9100

whenBad.title: You want to check up front whether auth and network would even work
whenBad.why: agent worker start connects live immediately, without a dry-run check first.
whenBad.alternative: Run agent worker debug first, then agent worker start

whenBad.title: Only a single, short agent run is needed
whenBad.why: agent worker start starts a long-running process connected to Cursor.
whenBad.alternative: Use a normal session with agent or agent -p instead of running a worker

commands.name: agent worker debug
commands.summary: Runs preflight diagnostics for the private worker (auth, privacy, routing).
commands.what: agent worker debug checks the private worker's authentication, privacy settings, and routing before actually starting it, and prints a diagnostic report. --json outputs the report as machine-readable JSON.

whenGood.title: Catch configuration mistakes before the first production start of a new worker
whenGood.example: agent worker debug --json

whenBad.title: The worker should just run in production directly, without a prior check
whenBad.why: agent worker debug doesn't start the worker, it only checks the prerequisites.
whenBad.alternative: Use agent worker start directly once the configuration is already known to work

commands.name: agent worker help [command]
commands.summary: Shows help for the worker subcommands.
commands.what: agent worker help [command] shows help for the worker subcommands (start, debug), optionally in detail for a specific subcommand.

whenGood.title: Look up the available flags of worker start, e.g. for pool configuration
whenGood.example: agent worker help start

whenBad.title: You just want to quickly see the options of the worker command you're already typing
whenBad.why: agent worker help command is a separate call.
