whenBad.alternative: Append -h or --help directly, e.g. agent worker start --help

commands.name: agent bedrock
commands.summary: Configures AWS Bedrock as a model backend with your own credentials or a team IAM role.
commands.what: Documented in the official changelog, not (yet) in the command reference table. Per the changelog entry (February 2026), agent bedrock configures access to AWS Bedrock either via your own access keys or a team IAM role, and works across interactive, headless, and editor sessions. As the slash command /bedrock it is additionally listed in the slash command reference doc; as a top-level CLI subcommand it's missing from reference/parameters.

whenGood.title: Set up Bedrock as a bring-your-own-key model backend instead of Cursor's default models
whenGood.example: agent bedrock

whenBad.title: You just want to switch the model for one run, not the backend
whenBad.why: agent bedrock configures the backend itself, not just a single model for one session.
whenBad.alternative: Use --model <model> or /model if you only need to pick a different already-available model

commands.name: Global Options (Flags)
commands.summary: Collects the most important global flags of the Cursor CLI that can be combined with any command.
commands.what:
Global options can be combined with any command (agent, agent mcp list, agent sandbox run, ...). The most important ones:
-p, --print: non-interactive mode, prints the response to the console (for scripts/CI); has full access to write and shell
--output-format <format>: text (default), json, or stream-json — only works together with --print
--model <model>: sets the model to use
--mode <mode>: plan or ask (default is full agent mode when this flag is omitted); --plan is shorthand for --mode=plan
-f, --force (alias --yolo): auto-allows commands unless explicitly denied — skips approval prompts
--sandbox <mode>: enabled or disabled
--approve-mcps: automatically approves all configured MCP servers
--trust: trusts the workspace without prompting (headless/print mode only)
--add-dir <path>: adds an extra directory for a multi-root session at startup (changelog-only)
--auto-review: enables auto-review execution mode, a classifier decides approvals instead of a fixed allowlist (changelog-only)
--image: attaches an image to the session (changelog-only)
-w, --worktree [name]: runs the agent in a new Git worktree instead of editing the current checkout directly
--resume [chatId] / --continue: resumes an existing or the most recently used chat session
--workspace <path>: sets the project directory to use
-h, --help: shows help for the given command

whenGood.title: Use -p in scripts and CI pipelines
whenGood.example: agent -p "find and fix performance issues" --model "gpt-5" --output-format json

whenGood.title: Combine flags deliberately instead of picking an unnecessarily broad mode
whenGood.example: agent -p --mode ask "summarize the auth module" — read-only, structured output for a script

whenBad.title: Set --force/--yolo by default on every run without reviewing the changes
whenBad.why: This also auto-approves risky commands without anyone seeing them beforehand.
whenBad.alternative: Review changes deliberately after the run (Ctrl+R or /changes), or additionally set --sandbox enabled to at least isolate execution

whenBad.title: Set --mode ask but then expect file changes afterwards
whenBad.why: In ask mode the agent makes no file changes; a script expecting write access afterwards will find nothing happened.
whenBad.alternative: Use default agent mode (no --mode flag) for runs that need file changes, or --plan to plan ahead first

commands.name: /add
commands.summary: Adds one or more files to the chat so Aider can edit them or use them as detailed context.
commands.what: /add adds files to the current chat session. Aider can only edit files that are in the chat. If a new file that doesn't exist yet is given to /add, Aider remembers the filename and writes to it later instead of accidentally changing an existing file. Besides the added files, Aider also uses a map of the whole git repo, so it's aware of code outside the chat too.

whenGood.title: Make a file editable
whenGood.example: /add payment.py — add the file to the chat before asking Aider to change it

whenGood.title: Safely create a new file
whenGood.example: /add utils/newfile.py before asking Aider to write a new helper function there — this way the change lands in the new file instead of an existing one

whenBad.title: Adding many files just in case
whenBad.why: Too much irrelevant code in the chat distracts the LLM; the docs recommend adding only the files actually needed.
whenBad.alternative: Add only the 1-2 truly relevant files and let the automatic repo map cover the rest

whenBad.title: Only wanting to show a file as reference, not edit it
whenBad.why: /add makes files directly editable — unnecessary if the file should only serve as context.
whenBad.alternative: Use /read-only to add the file for reference only

commands.name: /architect
commands.summary: Switches to architect mode, where one model plans the solution and a second model turns it into concrete file edits.
commands.what: In architect mode, Aider first sends the request to the main model (the architect), which proposes a solution. Aider then sends that proposal to a separate editor model (configurable with --editor-model), which turns it into concrete file edits. Without an attached prompt, /architect only switches to this mode persistently, without sending a message.

whenGood.title: Pair a strong reasoning model with a good editor model
whenGood.example: /architect Refactor the payment module to use the new API — e.g. with a strong reasoning model as architect and Sonnet or GPT-4o as the editor model

whenGood.title: Give the same model two passes at the problem
whenGood.example: /architect (no prompt, to switch persistently) — the model gets one pass to plan and one to implement, which the docs say can produce better results than plain code mode

whenBad.title: A quick, simple change
whenBad.why: Architect mode needs two LLM requests instead of one, which the docs note costs more time and money.
whenBad.alternative: Use /code for direct changes without the extra step

whenBad.title: Just discussing, not changing anything
whenBad.why: Architect mode ends up changing files; that's unnecessary if you only want to clarify questions.
whenBad.alternative: Use /ask to discuss the approach before any code gets changed

commands.name: /ask
commands.summary: Asks questions about the codebase without Aider changing any files.
commands.what: In ask mode, Aider answers questions about the code but never changes files. The docs describe a common workflow of bouncing between /ask and /code: discuss the plan with /ask first, then trigger the actual change with /code. Without an attached prompt, /ask only switches to the mode persistently.

whenGood.title: Discuss a plan before changing code
whenGood.example: /ask What's the best way to add caching to this function? — once you agree, switch to code mode and just say "go ahead"

whenGood.title: Understand the codebase without accidentally changing it
whenGood.example: /ask What does the retry logic in api_client.py do?

whenBad.title: Wanting an immediate code change
whenBad.why: The docs say ask mode never makes changes to files.
whenBad.alternative: Use /code for the actual implementation

whenBad.title: Asking about Aider itself (usage/configuration)
whenBad.why: /ask is meant for questions about the codebase, not about Aider as a tool.
whenBad.alternative: Use /help for questions about Aider's usage, configuration, or troubleshooting

commands.name: /chat-mode
commands.summary: Switches persistently (sticky) to any chat mode, given as an argument by name.
commands.what: /chat-mode <mode> switches the active mode for all following messages, e.g. /chat-mode code or /chat-mode architect. The commands /code, /ask and /architect do the same when called without a prompt; /help without a prompt only shows the basic help and does not switch the mode.

whenGood.title: Set the mode from a loaded command file
whenGood.example: /chat-mode ask inside a file that gets read with /load, to set the mode consistently before further commands run

whenBad.title: Directly targeting one of the known modes
whenBad.why: code/ask/architect have their own shorter commands with the same effect when called without a prompt.
whenBad.alternative: Use /code, /ask, or /architect directly

commands.name: /clear
commands.summary: Clears the chat history of the current session.
commands.what: /clear removes the previous conversation history from the chat context. The docs suggest this when Aider is stuck on a task and needs a fresh start.

whenGood.title: Fresh start when Aider is stuck on a task
whenGood.example: /clear — then rephrase the task more precisely

whenBad.title: Wanting to remove only individual files from context
whenBad.why: /clear wipes the whole conversation history but leaves files added to the chat untouched.
whenBad.alternative: Use /drop to remove specific files instead

commands.name: /code
commands.summary: Asks Aider to make concrete changes to the code (the default mode).
commands.what: /code is Aider's default mode: requests result in direct changes to the files in the chat. Without an attached prompt, /code only switches to this mode persistently, e.g. to return from ask or architect mode.

whenGood.title: Trigger implementation after an ask discussion
whenGood.example: /code go ahead and implement what we discussed

whenGood.title: Request a direct, clearly scoped change
whenGood.example: /code Add input validation to the signup form

whenBad.title: Wanting to clarify the approach before changing code
whenBad.why: /code implements immediately without discussing options first.
whenBad.alternative: Use /ask to discuss the plan first

whenBad.title: Wanting planning and implementation split across two models
whenBad.why: /code uses only one model for the whole request.
whenBad.alternative: Use /architect for the two-model workflow with a separate editor model

commands.name: /commit
commands.summary: Commits changes to the repo that were made outside the chat.
commands.what: /commit creates a commit for all dirty (uncommitted) changes in the repo, with a commit message generated by Aider. A custom commit message can optionally be given as an argument. Changes Aider makes itself are already committed automatically after every edit, per the docs.

whenGood.title: Save manual changes made outside Aider
whenGood.example: /commit — after editing something yourself outside Aider, before letting it keep working in the same repo

whenGood.title: Supply your own commit message
whenGood.example: /commit fix: correct off-by-one error in pagination

whenBad.title: Wanting to commit Aider's own code changes
whenBad.why: Aider already auto-commits its own edits after every change by default, so an extra /commit isn't needed for that.
whenBad.alternative: Only set --no-auto-commits if you deliberately want to turn off automatic committing

whenBad.title: Wanting to undo a bad commit
whenBad.why: /commit creates new commits, it doesn't undo anything.
whenBad.alternative: Use /undo to revert the last commit Aider made

commands.name: /context
commands.summary: Switches to context mode to see the surrounding code context for a request.
commands.what: In context mode, Aider shows the surrounding code context for a request. Without an attached prompt, /context only switches to this mode persistently.

whenGood.title: Understand the surroundings of a specific function
whenGood.example: /context Where else is the retry logic from api_client.py used?

whenGood.title: Keep working in context mode persistently
whenGood.example: /context (no prompt), so following messages are sent in this mode

whenBad.title: Wanting code actually changed
whenBad.why: The docs describe context mode as showing code context, not editing.
whenBad.alternative: Use /code or /architect for actual changes

whenBad.title: Wanting an overview of the whole repo instead of one spot
whenBad.why: /context is focused on the context around one specific request, not a full overview.
whenBad.alternative: Use /map for the complete repository map

commands.name: /copy
commands.summary: Copies Aider's last reply to the clipboard.
commands.what: /copy copies the most recently sent assistant message to the system clipboard.

whenGood.title: Reuse an explanation or code snippet from the last reply
whenGood.example: /copy — then paste it into e.g. docs, an issue, or another app

whenBad.title: Wanting to copy the whole code context for a web-chat LLM
whenBad.why: /copy only copies Aider's last reply, not the files added to the chat and the repo map.
whenBad.alternative: Use /copy-context to copy the full code context

commands.name: /copy-context
commands.summary: Copies the current code context (files, repo map, instructions) as markdown to the clipboard, suitable for pasting into a web chat UI.
commands.what: /copy-context <instructions> copies all files added via /add, all reference files added via /read-only, plus Aider's repository map and an instruction telling the LLM to output changes concisely. Any <instructions> appended are copied too. The docs describe this as a way to use a web-chat LLM as the "big brain" for planning, while Aider locally applies the edits with a cheaper model (via /paste of the reply).

whenGood.title: Use a capable web-chat LLM as architect without API access
whenGood.example: /copy-context Suggest how to add rate limiting to this API — paste the result into an LLM web chat UI when only web-chat, not API, access is available

whenGood.title: Share context with colleagues or in a ticket
whenGood.example: /copy-context — paste the resulting markdown into an issue or doc so others can follow the current code context

whenBad.title: Wanting to copy just the last Aider reply
whenBad.why: /copy-context copies files and the repo map, not the last chat reply.
whenBad.alternative: Use /copy for the last reply

whenBad.title: Wanting Aider to edit directly itself
whenBad.why: /copy-context only prepares a handoff to an external web-chat LLM; it doesn't change any files itself.
whenBad.alternative: Use /code or /architect if Aider should work directly with its configured model

sources.title: Aider Docs — Copy/paste with web chat

commands.name: /diff
commands.summary: Shows the diff of all file changes since the last chat message.
commands.what: /diff shows the changes made to the code since the last message sent.

whenGood.title: Check Aider's last change before continuing
whenGood.example: /diff — after a code change, quickly review exactly what was changed

whenGood.title: Understand what an auto-commit contained
whenGood.example: /diff right after an automatically committed change, to see its exact scope

whenBad.title: Wanting to undo an unwanted change
whenBad.why: /diff only displays changes, it doesn't change anything.
whenBad.alternative: Use /undo to revert the last Aider commit

whenBad.title: Wanting to see changes from several messages back
whenBad.why: The docs say /diff shows changes since the last message, not the whole session history.
whenBad.alternative: Use /git to run e.g. git log or git diff directly in the chat

commands.name: /drop
commands.summary: Removes files from the chat session to free up context space.
commands.what: /drop removes files previously added via /add or /read-only from the chat again. The docs describe it as normal to drop files that don't need further changes and add the files needed for the next step instead.

whenGood.title: Clean up after finishing a sub-step
whenGood.example: /drop payment.py — remove the file once it's done and no longer needed for the next step

whenGood.title: Aider is stuck, too much context in the chat
whenGood.example: /drop — the docs list this as one of the first things to try when Aider gets stuck on a task

whenBad.title: Wanting to reset the whole conversation history
whenBad.why: /drop only removes files from the chat, the conversation history stays intact.
whenBad.alternative: Use /clear for the chat history, or /reset to remove both at once

whenBad.title: Wanting to temporarily switch a file from editable to reference-only
whenBad.why: /drop removes the file from the chat entirely, so it's no longer available even as a reference afterward.
whenBad.alternative: Apply /read-only to the file to switch it to reference-only instead of dropping it completely

commands.name: /edit
commands.summary: Alias for /editor: opens an external editor to write a prompt.
commands.what: The docs list /edit as an alias for /editor, opening an external text editor to write the next chat message in.

whenGood.title: Comfortably write a longer, multi-line prompt
whenGood.example: /edit — opens the external editor instead of typing a long prompt directly into the terminal

whenBad.title: Writing a short one-line message
whenBad.why: For a single-line message, switching to an external editor is an unnecessary detour.
whenBad.alternative: Type the message directly into the terminal prompt

commands.name: /editor
commands.summary: Opens an external text editor to write the next chat message in.
commands.what: /editor opens an external editor for composing a prompt. The docs note this is also reachable via the Ctrl-X Ctrl-E keybinding directly in the terminal. Which editor opens can be set via the editor configuration.

whenGood.title: Write a multi-line prompt with precise formatting
whenGood.example: /editor — e.g. to cleanly format a prompt with code blocks or lists before sending it

whenBad.title: Sending a short, spontaneous message
whenBad.why: Switching to an external editor isn't worth it for a short message.
whenBad.alternative: Type the message directly into the chat prompt, or use /paste if the text is already on the clipboard

commands.name: /editor-model
commands.summary: Switches the editor model — responsible for writing concrete file edits in architect mode — to a new LLM.
commands.what: /editor-model changes the editor model during the running session. The docs describe the editor model as handling the second step in architect mode: turning the architect model's proposal into concrete file edits. Without a value, Aider automatically picks a suitable editor model for the main model; it can alternatively be set at startup with --editor-model.

whenGood.title: Use a better-suited editor model in architect mode
whenGood.example: /editor-model gpt-4o — after starting /architect with a strong reasoning model as the planner

whenGood.title: Swap the editor model mid-session without restarting
whenGood.example: /editor-model claude-sonnet — e.g. when the automatically chosen editor model isn't applying edits cleanly

whenBad.title: Wanting to change the main model
whenBad.why: The docs say /editor-model only affects the editor model in architect mode, not the model handling the request overall.
whenBad.alternative: Use /model to switch the main model

whenBad.title: Working outside architect mode
whenBad.why: The docs state the editor model is only used in architect mode.
whenBad.alternative: In code mode the main model is enough, no separate editor model is needed

commands.name: /exit
commands.summary: Exits Aider.
commands.what: /exit ends the running Aider application.

whenGood.title: Cleanly end a session
whenGood.example: /exit — when the work with Aider for this session is done

whenBad.title: Wanting to reset only the chat history and keep working
whenBad.why: /exit ends the whole program, not just the history.
whenBad.alternative: Use /clear to only clear the chat history and continue in the same session

commands.name: /git
commands.summary: Runs any git command; the output is excluded from the chat context.
commands.what: /git runs a raw git command (e.g. /git log or /git branch) without its output becoming part of the chat context. The docs describe this as being for more complex git history management beyond the built-in /diff, /undo, and /commit commands.

whenGood.title: Check git history or status directly from the chat
whenGood.example: /git log --oneline -5 — quickly review the last commits without leaving the chat

whenGood.title: Run a more complex git operation not covered by the built-in commands
whenGood.example: /git branch feature/checkout — create a new branch while continuing to work in Aider

whenBad.title: Wanting the output to become context for the LLM
whenBad.why: The docs say /git explicitly excludes its output from the chat context.
whenBad.alternative: Use /run instead, which offers to add its output to the chat

whenBad.title: Just wanting to commit or undo Aider's own changes
whenBad.why: There are dedicated, simpler commands for these standard cases.
whenBad.alternative: /commit to commit dirty changes, /undo to revert the last Aider commit

commands.name: /help
commands.summary: Answers questions about using, configuring, and troubleshooting Aider itself.
commands.what: /help answers questions about Aider as a tool, e.g. usage, configuration, and troubleshooting.

whenGood.title: Find out how to use an Aider feature
whenGood.example: /help How do I use aider with ollama?

whenBad.title: Asking questions about your own code or project
whenBad.why: The docs describe /help as answering questions about Aider itself, not about your codebase.
whenBad.alternative: Use /ask to ask questions about the code in the chat

commands.name: /lint
commands.summary: Lints and fixes the files in the chat, or all dirty files if none are in the chat.
commands.what: /lint checks the files currently in the chat with the configured linter (built in per language, or set via --lint-cmd) and has Aider fix any issues found. If no files are in the chat, all files with uncommitted changes (dirty files) get linted instead. The docs note Aider already auto-lints every file it edits itself by default.

whenGood.title: Check manually changed files before continuing
whenGood.example: /lint — after changing code yourself outside Aider, to check and fix the dirty files

whenGood.title: Lint specific files in the chat on demand
whenGood.example: /add payment.py followed by /lint, to check just that file

whenBad.title: Wanting to check tests instead of lint errors
whenBad.why: /lint only checks with the linter, not a test suite.
whenBad.alternative: Use /test <test-command> to run tests and have failures fixed

whenBad.title: Expecting to have to manually trigger linting after every Aider change
whenBad.why: The docs say Aider already auto-lints every file it edits by default; /lint is for the extra, manual case.
whenBad.alternative: Only set --no-auto-lint if automatic linting should be explicitly turned off

commands.name: /load
commands.summary: Loads a file of /commands and runs them one after another.
commands.what: /load reads in a file containing Aider /commands, one per line, and runs them in sequence.

whenGood.title: Automate a recurring sequence of commands
whenGood.example: /load setup.txt — read in and run a file with e.g. /add commands for a specific set of files

whenGood.title: Restore a file selection saved with /save
whenGood.example: /load session.txt — reconstruct a chat session's file selection that was previously saved with /save

whenBad.title: Running a single command directly
whenBad.why: The detour through a file is unnecessary for a single command.
whenBad.alternative: Type the command directly into the chat

whenBad.title: Wanting the command file loaded right at Aider's startup
whenBad.why: /load is an in-chat command for an already-running session.
whenBad.alternative: Use the --load LOAD_FILE flag at startup to have the file read in automatically

commands.name: /ls
commands.summary: Lists all known files and marks which ones are currently in the chat.
commands.what: /ls shows a list of all known files and marks which of them are currently added to the chat session.

whenGood.title: Get an overview of which files are currently in the chat
whenGood.example: /ls — quickly check what's currently active before the next /drop or /add

whenBad.title: Wanting to see a file's contents
whenBad.why: /ls only lists filenames and their chat status, not contents.
whenBad.alternative: Use /add or /read-only to actually bring a file's content into the chat

commands.name: /map
commands.summary: Prints the current repository map (an overview of the most important classes/functions in the repo).
commands.what: /map prints the current repository map: a compact overview of the most important classes and functions in the git repo, including type info and signatures, which Aider also uses itself as context for the LLM. The map's size is controlled by --map-tokens (default 1k tokens); /map-refresh can rebuild it manually.

whenGood.title: Get a quick overview of the project yourself
whenGood.example: /map — before deciding which files to add next with /add

whenGood.title: Check what Aider currently "sees" as context about the repo
whenGood.example: /map, to see which symbols from which files were included in the map

whenBad.title: Wanting to see a file's full source code
whenBad.why: The docs describe the repo map as containing only the most important symbols with signatures, not the full code.
whenBad.alternative: Open the file directly or add it to the chat with /add to see the full content

whenBad.title: Wanting an outdated map refreshed immediately
whenBad.why: /map shows the currently existing map but doesn't force a rebuild.
whenBad.alternative: Use /map-refresh to rebuild the repository map immediately

commands.name: /map-refresh
commands.summary: Forces an immediate rebuild of the repository map.
commands.what: /map-refresh immediately rebuilds the repository map (the compact overview of symbols and structure in the project that Aider gives the model as context), instead of waiting for the automatic refresh. How often the map otherwise refreshes automatically is controlled by --map-refresh (auto/always/files/manual, default auto). The map size itself is controlled with --map-tokens (0 disables it entirely).

whenGood.title: After large manual changes made outside the chat
whenGood.example: /map-refresh after several new files were added via a git branch switch or merge, so Aider picks them up in the map

whenGood.title: Map seems stale even though --map-refresh is set to manual
whenGood.example: /map-refresh right before a complex request, to make sure the symbol overview is current

whenBad.title: The repo has no relevant changes
whenBad.why: Rebuilding costs time and, depending on project size, noticeable CPU, without changing the result.
whenBad.alternative: Rely on the automatic refresh (--map-refresh auto, the default)

whenBad.title: The repository map is disabled anyway
whenBad.why: With --map-tokens 0 there is no map to refresh.
whenBad.alternative: Set --map-tokens to a value above 0 to actually use the map

commands.name: /model
commands.summary: Switches the Main Model of the running Aider session to a new LLM.
commands.what: /model <name> switches the main model that generates Aider's suggestions and code changes, for the running session. Without an argument, Aider shows a list of matching models. The main model can also be set directly at startup with --model (env var AIDER_MODEL); in architect mode it's the planning model, while --editor-model separately writes the file edits.

whenGood.title: Upgrade the model mid-session for a harder subtask
whenGood.example: /model anthropic/claude-opus-4 — before a complex multi-file refactor

whenGood.title: Switch provider without restarting the session
whenGood.example: /model gemini/gemini-2.5-pro — when a different API key is cheaper or available right now

whenBad.title: The model should already be fixed for the whole session
whenBad.why: Switching with /model later doesn't reset the model context automatically; if you already know the model, there's no need to start and then switch.
whenBad.alternative: Set --model directly at startup, e.g. aider --model anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-5

whenBad.title: Just want to briefly try a different model without losing session state
whenBad.why: /model switches permanently for the rest of the session and also changes subsequent answers.
whenBad.alternative: Start a second Aider instance with --model in another terminal to compare in parallel

commands.name: /models
commands.summary: Searches the list of models Aider supports for a name or partial name.
commands.what: /models <search term> lists all known model names matching the input text, including the provider prefix (e.g. anthropic/, gemini/, openrouter/). This helps find the exact model string for /model or --model. Without a search term, the command shows usage hints. On the command line, --list-models MODEL (alias --models) does the same.

whenGood.title: Find the exact model string before calling /model
whenGood.example: /models sonnet — finds anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-5 among others

whenGood.title: Check whether a new model is already known to Aider
whenGood.example: /models gemini-3 — shows whether and under which name a new Gemini model is available

whenBad.title: The exact model name is already known
whenBad.why: A search is an unnecessary extra step if the provider/model string is already fixed.
whenBad.alternative: Use /model <known-name> or --model <known-name> directly

whenBad.title: You only want to see the currently active model, not all available ones
whenBad.why: /models searches the whole model list but doesn't show which model is currently active.
whenBad.alternative: Use /settings to see the currently effective configuration, including the active model

commands.name: /multiline-mode
commands.summary: Toggles multiline input mode, which swaps the behavior of Enter and Meta+Enter.
commands.what: Normally Enter sends the message and Meta+Enter (or Esc+Enter) inserts a new line. /multiline-mode swaps that: Enter then inserts a new line, and Meta+Enter sends the message. Useful when pasting or writing longer, multi-line prompts. The same mode can also be enabled permanently at startup with the --multiline switch.

whenGood.title: Type longer code snippets or error messages directly
whenGood.example: /multiline-mode, then paste a multi-line stack trace without special characters like {..} and send with Meta+Enter

whenGood.title: Want to work in multiline mode permanently
whenGood.example: aider --multiline at startup, instead of manually toggling /multiline-mode every time

whenBad.title: Just writing a single short message
whenBad.why: In multiline mode Enter no longer sends the message; you have to remember Meta+Enter too — unnecessary friction for short prompts.
whenBad.alternative: Stay in the default mode and use {tag} tags or /editor for the occasional multi-line case

whenBad.title: Unsure whether Enter currently sends or inserts a line
whenBad.why: After switching, Enter behaves differently than usual, which easily leads to accidentally sent or stuck input.
whenBad.alternative: Check the active mode via /settings or the prompt behavior before important input

commands.name: /ok
commands.summary: Shorthand to confirm proposed changes in code mode.
commands.what: /ok is shorthand for '/code Ok, please go ahead and make those changes.'. Any extra text typed after it is appended to that prompt.

whenGood.title: Quickly approve a proposal from an ask/architect discussion
whenGood.example: /ok — instead of typing out the whole confirmation sentence yourself

whenBad.title: You still want to add change requests, not just agree
whenBad.why: /ok only appends your text to a fixed agreement sentence, it doesn't replace a precise instruction of your own.
whenBad.alternative: Use /code <your own, concrete instruction>

commands.name: /paste
commands.summary: Pastes an image or text from the clipboard into the chat.
commands.what: /paste reads the current clipboard content (image or text) and adds it as a message to the chat. For images, an optional name can be given for the image.

whenGood.title: Insert a screenshot of a bug or UI state
whenGood.example: Copy a screenshot, then /paste bug.png — Aider receives the image as context

whenBad.title: The clipboard content is just plain text that could be typed or pasted directly
whenBad.why: /paste is mainly meant for images, or when direct pasting into the terminal input doesn't work.
whenBad.alternative: Paste text directly into the chat input line with normal copy-paste

commands.name: /quit
commands.summary: Exits Aider (equivalent to /exit).
commands.what: /quit immediately ends the running Aider session, exactly like /exit. It's a pure alias with no extra behavior.

whenGood.title: Cleanly end the session instead of force-closing the terminal window
whenGood.example: /quit after all desired changes have been committed

whenBad.title: You only want to reset files/history but keep working
whenBad.why: /quit ends the whole process, not just the current chat selection.
whenBad.alternative: Use /reset to clear files and history without leaving Aider

commands.name: /read-only
commands.summary: Adds files to the chat for reference only, without letting Aider edit them.
commands.what: /read-only <file> adds a file to the chat as read-only, so Aider can read it for context but never edit it directly. Files already added with /add can be switched to read-only afterwards with /read-only. On the command line, this corresponds to the --read FILE flag (usable multiple times).

whenGood.title: Give an interface or config file as context without risking accidental edits
whenGood.example: /read-only src/types.ts, so Aider knows the types but never edits them itself

whenGood.title: Provide a large reference file (e.g. API docs as markdown) as context
whenGood.example: /read-only docs/api-reference.md before a question about correct endpoint usage

whenBad.title: The file actually needs to be changed
whenBad.why: Aider explicitly never edits read-only files, even if the request sounds like it should.
whenBad.alternative: Use /add <file> to make it editable

whenBad.title: Adding many large reference files read-only that are barely needed
whenBad.why: Even read-only files consume context space (tokens) on every request.
whenBad.alternative: Only add the files actually needed, and remove them with /drop once no longer needed

commands.name: /reasoning-effort
commands.summary: Sets the reasoning effort level of the current model (e.g. low/medium/high).
commands.what: /reasoning-effort <value> sets the reasoning_effort API parameter for models that support adjustable reasoning (e.g. OpenAI's o-series/GPT-5 series). Depending on the model, a number or a level like low/medium/high is expected. On the command line this corresponds to --reasoning-effort VALUE; by default nothing is set (the model's own default applies).

whenGood.title: Force more thinking time for a difficult debugging task
whenGood.example: /reasoning-effort high before a tricky bug hunt in concurrent code

whenGood.title: Lower cost/latency for simple routine tasks
whenGood.example: /reasoning-effort low before Aider only needs to adjust comments or formatting

whenBad.title: The current model doesn't support adjustable reasoning at all
whenBad.why: Many models simply ignore the parameter, or the API rejects it.
whenBad.alternative: Check the model docs or /models to see whether reasoning_effort is supported; otherwise pick a different model

whenBad.title: Wanting to always work with maximum reasoning by default
whenBad.why: High reasoning effort increases latency and token cost even for tasks that don't need it.
whenBad.alternative: Set effort project-wide via config and only raise it per /reasoning-effort for individual hard tasks

commands.name: /report
commands.summary: Reports a problem by automatically preparing a GitHub issue in the Aider repository.
commands.what: /report opens a pre-filled GitHub issue draft in the browser (repo Aider-AI/aider), including relevant context about the problem encountered, to make it easier to report bugs.

whenGood.title: A reproducible bug or crash in Aider itself
whenGood.example: /report after a crash in the middle of a session, to report it to the Aider maintainers

whenBad.title: The problem is in your own project code, not in Aider
whenBad.why: /report creates an issue in the Aider repository, not in your own project repo.
whenBad.alternative: For issues in your own code, keep working with Aider normally or open an issue in your own project repo

commands.name: /reset
commands.summary: Removes all files from the chat and also clears the entire chat history.
commands.what: /reset combines /drop (remove all files from the chat session) and /clear (clear the chat history) into one command. Afterwards the session starts fresh content-wise, but existing git commits and files on disk are untouched.

whenGood.title: Start a completely new topic in the same Aider window
whenGood.example: /reset to switch to a fully unrelated bugfix in a different part of the codebase after finishing a feature

whenGood.title: The chat context has become cluttered/confusing after many iterations
whenGood.example: /reset when conflicting earlier instructions are visibly degrading answer quality

whenBad.title: Only a few no-longer-needed files should go, but the conversation should stay
whenBad.why: /reset also clears the entire chat history, not just the file list.
whenBad.alternative: Use /drop <file> for individual files

whenBad.title: The previous conversation contains important context still needed
whenBad.why: After /reset Aider no longer knows earlier decisions and rationale from the chat.
whenBad.alternative: Save relevant points with /save before resetting, or manually copy the chat history into notes

commands.name: /run
commands.summary: Runs a shell command and offers to add its output to the chat.
commands.what: /run <command> (alias: !) runs any shell command in the project directory and shows the output. Afterwards Aider asks whether the output should be added to the chat context, so the model can react to, e.g., errors or test results.

whenGood.title: Quickly kick off a build or test command and discuss the result right away
whenGood.example: /run npm run build — add the output to the chat on failure so Aider fixes the error immediately

whenGood.title: Check project state without switching to another terminal
whenGood.example: /run git status — quickly see what changed since the last commit

whenBad.title: The same test command should run automatically after every change
whenBad.why: /run has to be called manually each time; it's not a persistent hook.
whenBad.alternative: Use --test-cmd together with --auto-test, or /test, for recurring test runs with automatic error output in the chat

whenBad.title: A potentially destructive command (e.g. a database migration, rm) should run
whenBad.why: /run executes the command unchecked in the real project directory; a typo can cause real damage.
whenBad.alternative: Test risky commands directly in your own terminal with full control first, before running them via /run

commands.name: /save
commands.summary: Saves the current file selection of the chat session as a command list in a file.
commands.what: /save <filename> writes the necessary /add and /read-only commands into a file, which can later restore the chat session's current file setup. /load <filename> restores it by running the saved commands in order.

whenGood.title: Save a recurring working set of files for a specific feature
whenGood.example: /save auth-feature.txt after assembling the relevant auth files with /add

whenGood.title: Share the setup with a teammate
whenGood.example: /save review-setup.txt and commit the file to the repo, so others can load the same file context with /load review-setup.txt

whenBad.title: The entire chat history including answers should be saved
whenBad.why: /save only saves the file selection (which /add/--read-only commands are needed), not the conversation content.
whenBad.alternative: Use the chat history file (default .aider.chat.history.md, configurable via --chat-history-file) for the conversation

whenBad.title: The file list changes almost every session anyway
whenBad.why: The saved snapshot then goes stale quickly and needs constant regenerating.
whenBad.alternative: Just add files directly with /add each session instead of the save/load detour

commands.name: /settings
commands.summary: Prints the currently effective Aider settings.
commands.what: /settings shows an overview of the session's currently active configuration: among other things the main, weak, and editor model, edit format, active chat mode, and other effective options from flags, config file, and environment variables.

whenGood.title: Check which model/edit format is actually active right now
whenGood.example: /settings after several /model and /chat-mode switches, to confirm the current state

whenGood.title: Narrow down the cause of unexpected behavior
whenGood.example: /settings to check whether a config file or env variable overrode an option

whenBad.title: You want to change settings, not just view them
whenBad.why: /settings is purely informational and changes nothing.
whenBad.alternative: Use the relevant commands like /model, /weak-model, or /chat-mode for the actual change

whenBad.title: The question is only about token usage, not general configuration
whenBad.why: /settings shows configuration values, not a token count of the current context.
whenBad.alternative: Use /tokens for a report on the current context's token usage

commands.name: /test
commands.summary: Runs a test command and automatically adds its output to the chat on failure.
commands.what: /test <command> runs a shell command (typically the test suite). Unlike /run, the output is only added to the chat automatically on a non-zero exit code, so Aider can work directly with the test failures. Without a command, Aider uses the one configured via --test-cmd; --test runs it once at startup and then exits; --auto-test repeats it automatically after every change.

whenGood.title: Check right after a change whether tests still pass
whenGood.example: /test pytest -q — on failure, the traceback lands in the chat automatically so Aider can fix it

whenGood.title: Automate the test run permanently after every Aider change
whenGood.example: Set --test-cmd 'npm test' together with --auto-test at startup instead of calling /test manually all the time

whenBad.title: You want to see the output every time, even on a successful run
whenBad.why: /test only adds the output to the chat automatically on failure (non-zero exit).
whenBad.alternative: Use /run <test command> and add the output to the chat manually when it's also needed on success

whenBad.title: The command isn't a test command but e.g. a build or deploy script
whenBad.why: /test is semantically meant for test commands; a non-zero exit from a deploy script can trigger the wrong error interpretation.
whenBad.alternative: Use /run for general shell commands

commands.name: /think-tokens
commands.summary: Sets the model's thinking-token budget for extended reasoning.
commands.what: /think-tokens <value> sets how many tokens a model with extended thinking (e.g. Claude models with thinking support) may use for its internal reasoning process. Values can be given compactly, e.g. 8096, 8k, 10.5k, or 0.5M; 0 disables thinking. On the command line this corresponds to --thinking-tokens VALUE.

whenGood.title: Give more thinking budget for an architecturally complex question
whenGood.example: /think-tokens 16k before a question about redesigning the data model layer

whenGood.title: Turn thinking off specifically to get faster/cheaper answers
whenGood.example: /think-tokens 0 for simple, repetitive changes across many small files

whenBad.title: The active model doesn't support extended thinking
whenBad.why: The parameter only has an effect on models with a matching thinking feature, otherwise it does nothing.
whenBad.alternative: Use /reasoning-effort for models that instead support reasoning_effort levels (e.g. OpenAI's o-/GPT-5 series)

whenBad.title: Setting a very high thinking budget by default for every request
whenBad.why: An unnecessarily high budget increases latency and cost even when the task is trivial.
whenBad.alternative: Only raise the budget for genuinely demanding requests, then lower it again

commands.name: /tokens
commands.summary: Shows a report on how many tokens the current chat context is using.
commands.what: /tokens lists how many tokens each part of the current context takes up (e.g. system prompt, repository map, added files, chat history), including a total. This helps estimate how close you are to a context limit.

whenGood.title: Check what's filling up the context the most right now
whenGood.example: /tokens to see whether a large read-only file is taking up a disproportionate amount of space

whenGood.title: Check headroom in the context window before an especially long request
whenGood.example: /tokens before a big multi-file change, to see whether files should be dropped first with /drop

whenBad.title: You want to actively change the repository map size, not just see usage
whenBad.why: /tokens only shows the current state, it doesn't change any settings.
whenBad.alternative: Set --map-tokens to actually change the map size

whenBad.title: The context is already noticeably too full and responses are breaking
whenBad.why: Just looking at the report doesn't fix the problem yet.
whenBad.alternative: Directly remove no-longer-needed files with /drop, or use /clear for the history

commands.name: /undo
commands.summary: Undoes the last git commit, provided it was made by Aider.
commands.what: /undo runs a git reset on the last commit, but only if that commit was made by Aider itself (Aider recognizes its own commits). This lets you specifically revert unwanted automatic changes without accidentally losing your own manual commits.

whenGood.title: Aider's last change didn't solve the problem or broke something
whenGood.example: /undo right after a failed fix, to go back to the previous state and phrase the request more precisely

whenGood.title: Compare two alternative solution approaches
whenGood.example: Have proposal A implemented, check the result, /undo, then try proposal B

whenBad.title: The last commit wasn't made by Aider (e.g. made manually)
whenBad.why: /undo deliberately refuses to reset when the last commit wasn't made by Aider, to protect other people's work.
whenBad.alternative: Run git reset manually in your own terminal, fully aware of the consequences

whenBad.title: Multiple Aider commits need to be undone
whenBad.why: /undo only reverts the single most recent commit, not several at once.
whenBad.alternative: Call /undo repeatedly, or reset directly to an earlier commit hash with git reset

commands.name: /voice
commands.summary: Records spoken input and transcribes it as a message in the chat.
commands.what: /voice starts an audio recording via the microphone and then transcribes the speech into text, which is sent as a chat message. Format (--voice-format, default wav; webm/mp3 need ffmpeg) and language (--voice-language, ISO-639-1 code, default en) are configurable.

whenGood.title: Dictate a longer request faster than typing it
whenGood.example: /voice to freely describe a detailed request for a new feature

whenGood.title: Work hands-free, e.g. while reading code on a second screen
whenGood.example: Use /voice to speak observations directly instead of switching between reading and typing

whenBad.title: Precise code identifiers, file paths, or special characters need to be exact
whenBad.why: Speech recognition often mis-transcribes technical terms, CamelCase names, or paths.
whenBad.alternative: Type such details directly, or insert them via /paste instead of speaking them

whenBad.title: No working microphone or supported audio environment is available
whenBad.why: /voice needs microphone access and, for some formats, ffmpeg; without that, recording fails.
whenBad.alternative: Type the text normally, or paste it from the clipboard with /paste

commands.name: /weak-model
commands.summary: Switches the Weak Model responsible for commit messages and chat history summarization.
commands.what: /weak-model <name> sets the so-called weak model: a cheaper/faster secondary model that Aider uses internally for side tasks like writing commit messages and summarizing older chat history, while the main model (/model) handles the actual code changes. On the command line this corresponds to --weak-model WEAK_MODEL.

whenGood.title: Cut costs without hurting the quality of the actual code answers
whenGood.example: /weak-model anthropic/claude-haiku-4-5, while the main model stays a more capable model

whenGood.title: Use a different model when the default doesn't produce good commit messages
whenGood.example: Switch /weak-model to another model when the current weak model's generated commit messages are imprecise

whenBad.title: You want to improve the quality of the actual code answers
whenBad.why: The weak model only affects side tasks (commit messages, history summarization), not the code suggestions themselves.
whenBad.alternative: Use /model to switch the main model

whenBad.title: No deliberate reason for a second, weaker model (e.g. a solo setup with only one model available anyway)
whenBad.why: An unnecessarily weak weak-model can, e.g., lead to worse commit messages without any real cost benefit if only one model is available anyway.
whenBad.alternative: Leave the weak model at Aider's sensible default

commands.name: /web
commands.summary: Scrapes a web page, converts it to markdown, and sends it as a message in the chat.
commands.what: /web <URL> fetches a web page, converts its content to markdown, and adds it as a chat message, so the model can use, e.g., external documentation or web-based error reports as context. Images on the page can also be picked up, provided the model supports image input.

whenGood.title: Provide external API documentation as context for an integration
whenGood.example: /web https://docs.example.com/api/v2 before a question about correct endpoint usage

whenGood.title: Bring in an error message/issue discussion found on the web
whenGood.example: /web <GitHub issue URL>, to bring a known solution in as context for your own bug

whenBad.title: The page requires login or is heavily JavaScript-driven/interactive
whenBad.why: /web scrapes static page content; content behind logins or loaded purely dynamically is often not captured correctly.
whenBad.alternative: Copy the relevant text manually and paste it via /paste or directly into the chat input

whenBad.title: The web page is very long and only a small section is relevant
whenBad.why: The full page as markdown takes up unnecessary context (tokens).
whenBad.alternative: Manually copy and paste only the relevant excerpt instead of the whole page via /web

commands.name: Code Mode
commands.summary: Default mode: Aider directly changes files to carry out the request.
commands.what: In code mode (the default at startup), Aider makes direct changes to project files to carry out the request — the classic 'just do it' mode. Switch permanently with /chat-mode code or /code (no prompt), or for a single message with /code <request> regardless of the currently active mode. At startup, the mode can be forced with --chat-mode code (though it's the default anyway).

whenGood.title: The desired solution approach is already clear
whenGood.example: /code Add rate-limit middleware to the Express route /api/login

whenGood.title: Quickly push through a series of small, clearly scoped changes
whenGood.example: Stay in code mode for a series of small bugfixes, without switching modes every time

whenBad.title: First just discuss options and trade-offs before changing anything
whenBad.why: In code mode, Aider treats requests as an order to change files immediately, not as an open discussion.
whenBad.alternative: Use /ask to discuss first, then implement with /code

whenBad.title: A weaker model needs to both propose AND implement complex changes in one step
whenBad.why: Some models are good at proposing but weaker at precisely editing files in a single request.
whenBad.alternative: Use architect mode, which splits planning and file edits across two models

commands.name: Ask Mode
commands.summary: Discussion mode: Aider discusses the code and answers questions, but never changes files.
commands.what: In ask mode, Aider answers questions about the codebase and discusses approaches, but makes no file changes. Recommended workflow: discuss options in /ask first and agree on a plan, then trigger the actual implementation with /code (often a short 'go ahead' is enough). Switch permanently with /chat-mode ask or /ask (no prompt), or for a single message with /ask <question>. Startup flag: --chat-mode ask.

whenGood.title: Understand how part of the codebase works before changing anything
whenGood.example: /ask How is authentication structured in this project?

whenGood.title: Weigh multiple solution options before touching files
whenGood.example: /ask What would be two sensible approaches to add rate limiting? — then have the chosen approach implemented with /code

whenBad.title: The plan is already set, it should be implemented directly
whenBad.why: In ask mode Aider never changes files as a matter of principle, even if the request sounds like an implementation order.
whenBad.alternative: Use /code, or switch to code mode

whenBad.title: You want Aider to explain its own usage/configuration, not the project
whenBad.why: /ask refers to the loaded project codebase, not to Aider itself.
whenBad.alternative: Use /help for questions about Aider's usage, configuration, and troubleshooting

commands.name: Architect Mode
commands.summary: Two-model mode: one model plans the change, a second editor model turns it into concrete file edits.
commands.what: In architect mode, Aider first sends the request to the main model (the architect), which proposes a solution; a separate editor model (--editor-model, otherwise chosen automatically based on the main model) then translates that proposal into concrete file edits. By default the edits are applied automatically (--auto-accept-architect, default True). Especially useful with models that are strong at reasoning but weaker at precise file editing (per Aider's docs, e.g. OpenAI's o1 models, paired with an editor model like GPT-4o or Sonnet). Activate via /chat-mode architect, /architect (no prompt for a permanent switch, with a prompt for a single message), or at startup with --architect or --chat-mode architect.

whenGood.title: Pair a strong reasoning model as architect with a separate, edit-strong model
whenGood.example: aider --architect --model o1 --editor-model anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-5 for a complex architectural decision followed by precise implementation

whenGood.title: Get better results on a hard change even with just one model
whenGood.example: /architect for a complex change where two separate LLM requests (planning, then editing) yield better results than a single step in code mode

whenBad.title: Simple, unambiguous changes where planning and editing clearly coincide
whenBad.why: Architect mode needs two LLM requests instead of one, which costs time and money — unnecessary for trivial changes.
whenBad.alternative: Stay in default code mode

whenBad.title: Proposed changes should be reviewed manually before landing in files
whenBad.why: With the default --auto-accept-architect, the edits produced by the editor model are applied automatically.
whenBad.alternative: Set --no-auto-accept-architect to confirm every change manually

commands.name: Help Mode
commands.summary: Help mode: Aider answers questions about itself — usage, configuration, troubleshooting.
commands.what: In help mode, Aider answers questions about its own usage, configuration, and troubleshooting, instead of referring to the loaded project codebase. Switch permanently with /chat-mode help, or for a single message with /help <question> regardless of the currently active mode. Startup flag: --chat-mode help.

whenGood.title: Unclear how to use a specific Aider feature
whenGood.example: /help How do I set up a custom lint command for Python?

whenGood.title: Make sense of an error message or unexpected behavior from Aider itself
whenGood.example: /help Why is Aider reporting a token limit even though the model should support more context?

whenBad.title: The question is about your own project code, not Aider itself
whenBad.why: /help refers to Aider's own docs/configuration, not the loaded codebase.
whenBad.alternative: Use /ask for questions about your own project

whenBad.title: A concrete change should be implemented directly
whenBad.why: Help mode only answers questions, it makes no file changes.
whenBad.alternative: Use /code to actually implement the change

commands.name: CLI Options (Flags)
commands.summary: Collects the most important Aider CLI flags, grouped by model, automation, context, and other settings.
commands.what:
Aider's CLI flags can be combined at startup (e.g. aider --model ... --test-cmd ... --yes-always) or set via a config file/environment variables. The most important ones, grouped:
Model: --model MODEL (main model, env AIDER_MODEL); --weak-model WEAK_MODEL (for commit messages/history summarization); --editor-model EDITOR_MODEL (writes the file edits in architect mode); --architect (activates architect mode directly); --reasoning-effort VALUE and --thinking-tokens VALUE (control reasoning depth/budget)
Automation/Scripting: --message/-m/--msg (send a single message, process the reply, then exit - for scripts/CI); --message-file/-f (read the message from a file); --yes-always (auto-confirms every prompt without asking); --no-auto-commits (disables automatic committing of LLM changes, default is on); --test-cmd (define a test command) together with --auto-test or a one-off --test; --lint-cmd (lint commands per language) together with --auto-lint or a one-off --lint; --watch-files (watches files for special AI comments that trigger Aider)
Context: --read FILE (add a file read-only, usable multiple times) or --file FILE (add it editable); --map-tokens VALUE (repository map size, 0 disables it); --map-refresh VALUE (auto/always/files/manual)
Install/Other: --upgrade/--update (update Aider itself); --just-check-update (only check whether an update is available); --config/-c (custom config file); --env-file (custom .env file for API keys); --version; --help/-h

whenGood.title: Use -m in scripts/CI pipelines for non-interactive single runs
whenGood.example: aider --message 'Fix the failing test in test_auth.py' --yes-always --test-cmd 'pytest -q' in a CI job

whenGood.title: Scope automation deliberately instead of allowing everything
whenGood.example: aider --test-cmd 'pytest -q' --auto-test --no-auto-commits — tests run automatically, but commits only after manual review

whenBad.title: Setting --yes-always by default on every interactive run without reviewing the suggestions
whenBad.why: This also auto-confirms risky or unwanted changes without anyone seeing them first.
whenBad.alternative: Only use --yes-always in controlled script/CI contexts with --message; confirm suggestions manually when interactive

whenBad.title: Letting API keys end up as plaintext flags in shell history
whenBad.why: --openai-api-key/--anthropic-api-key as a command-line argument often stays visible in shell history and process lists.
whenBad.alternative: Set keys via --env-file/.env or environment variables like AIDER_ANTHROPIC_API_KEY instead

commands.name: agy
commands.summary: Starts the Antigravity CLI TUI in the current working directory.
commands.what: agy with no arguments opens the interactive terminal interface (TUI) in the current directory. On first launch in a directory with an existing Gemini CLI configuration, the CLI detects it automatically and offers an interactive migration checklist. Sign-in goes through the OS keyring (Keychain/Secret Service/Credential Manager); if no session is saved, it opens your default browser automatically. Over SSH without a local browser, the CLI instead prints an authorization URL to open in your local browser plus a code to paste back into the terminal.

whenGood.title: Start a new project interactively
whenGood.example: Run agy in the project folder to open the TUI and work with the agent in a dialogue

whenGood.title: Switch over from Gemini CLI
whenGood.example: Run agy in a folder with an existing Gemini CLI configuration to get the automatic migration checklist

whenBad.title: You want a single command to run without a TUI, e.g. in a script
whenBad.why: agy with no flags always opens the full interactive interface and blocks the terminal.
whenBad.alternative: agy -p / --print for non-interactive execution

sources.title: Antigravity CLI docs: migration from Gemini CLI

commands.name: agy -p / --print
commands.summary: Runs a prompt non-interactively and exits afterward (print/headless mode).
commands.what: agy -p or --print "<prompt>" runs a single prompt without the TUI, suited for git hooks, scripts, and shell pipelines. Since 1.1.1 the mode writes server errors to stderr and returns a non-zero exit code instead of silently exiting empty; since 1.1.2 it hard-fails with a non-zero exit code and a list of available models when --model can't be resolved, instead of silently falling back to the default model. It can be combined with -c or --conversation <id> to continue an existing conversation.

whenGood.title: Wire Antigravity into a script or git hook
whenGood.example: agy -p "Review this git diff and draft a conventional commit message" --cwd $(pwd)

whenGood.title: Continue a previous conversation non-interactively
whenGood.example: agy -c -p "summarize where things stand"

whenBad.title: You want to steer the run interactively or answer follow-up questions live
whenBad.why: Print mode runs straight through with no TUI and no interactive follow-up questions.
whenBad.alternative: Start agy without -p for the normal interactive TUI

commands.name: agy -c / --continue
commands.summary: Instantly resumes the last active conversation for the current workspace.
commands.what: agy -c or --continue loads the most recently used conversation for the current working directory directly, without opening the /resume picker. The CLI keeps track of the last conversation ID per directory in a cache file. It can be combined with -p to continue an existing conversation non-interactively.

whenGood.title: Quickly keep working on the same task in the same folder
whenGood.example: Run agy -c right after restarting in the same project folder

whenGood.title: Continue an existing conversation non-interactively
whenGood.example: agy -c -p "what's still missing?"

whenBad.title: You want to choose from several saved conversations
whenBad.why: -c always loads only the directory's most recently active conversation, with no picker.
whenBad.alternative: Use /resume inside a running agy session, or agy --conversation <id> for a specific ID

commands.name: agy --conversation <id>
commands.summary: Loads a specific conversation directly by its ID, without a picker.
commands.what: agy --conversation <id> loads a specific saved conversation by its unique ID, skipping the interactive /resume picker. It can be combined with -p to continue that conversation non-interactively; since 1.0.9 this combined mode prints only the newly generated response instead of the entire historical transcript.

whenGood.title: Jump back to a known conversation ID directly
whenGood.example: agy --conversation 7f3a2b1c

whenGood.title: Continue a specific conversation non-interactively
whenGood.example: agy --conversation 7f3a2b1c -p "summarize"

whenBad.title: You don't know the exact conversation ID
whenBad.why: --conversation needs an exact ID; the flag has no fuzzy search.
whenBad.alternative: Use /resume inside a running agy session to browse and pick a conversation

commands.name: agy --model <name>
commands.summary: Sets which model to use at startup.
commands.what: agy --model <name> sets the reasoning model when starting the CLI. Interactive sessions fall back to the default model with a warning if the model can't be resolved; in print mode (-p) the CLI instead hard-fails since 1.1.2 with a non-zero exit code and a list of available models. Available names come from the agy models subcommand.

whenGood.title: Start directly with a specific model
whenGood.example: agy --model="Gemini 3.1 Pro"

whenGood.title: Make sure a script uses exactly the intended model
whenGood.example: agy --model <name> -p "<prompt>" hard-fails on a wrong name instead of silently using a different model

whenBad.title: The model choice should persist for all future sessions
whenBad.why: --model only applies to the current launch, not persistently.
whenBad.alternative: Use /model inside a running agy session, which persists the model across sessions

commands.name: agy models
commands.summary: Lists the available models.
commands.what: agy models (subcommand) shows the models available for the current plan. According to the docs, Gemini 3.1 Pro and Gemini 3.5 Flash are available on every plan; Claude Sonnet 4.6 (thinking), Claude Opus 4.6 (thinking) and GPT-OSS-120b are Google AI Ultra only. The listed model name can be passed straight to --model.

whenGood.title: Look up the exact model name before starting
whenGood.example: agy models to find the exact name to pass to --model

whenBad.title: You want to switch the model for the running session
whenBad.why: agy models is a plain listing subcommand outside a running session.
whenBad.alternative: Use /model inside a running agy session

commands.name: agy --agent <name>
commands.summary: Selects a custom agent at startup.
commands.what: agy --agent <name> (since 1.1.1) selects a custom agent instead of the default agent when starting the CLI. Custom agents can be listed beforehand with the agy agent / agy agents subcommand.

whenGood.title: Start with a specialized custom agent instead of the default one
whenGood.example: agy --agent code-reviewer

whenBad.title: You don't know the name of the custom agent you want
whenBad.why: --agent expects an existing, exact agent name.
whenBad.alternative: Run agy agents to list them first, then set --agent <name>

commands.name: agy agent / agy agents
commands.summary: Lists the available custom agents.
commands.what: agy agent or agy agents (subcommand, since 1.1.1) shows the available custom agents. The name it returns can be passed straight to --agent at startup; inside a running session, the /agents panel takes over agent selection and subagent monitoring.

whenGood.title: Look up available custom agents before starting
whenGood.example: agy agents to find the exact name to pass to --agent

whenBad.title: You're already in a running session and want to manage agents or monitor subagents
whenBad.why: The subcommand only gives a one-off list outside the TUI.
whenBad.alternative: Use /agents inside a running agy session

commands.name: agy --project <name>
commands.summary: Explicitly sets which project to use at startup.
commands.what: agy --project <name> (since 1.0.12) explicitly sets which project to use at startup, instead of deriving the project automatically from the active workspace. Complements --new-project, which creates a new project instead.

whenGood.title: Pick an existing project independent of the current directory
whenGood.example: agy --project my-project

whenBad.title: You want a brand-new project to be created
whenBad.why: --project only picks among existing projects; it doesn't create one.
whenBad.alternative: Use agy --new-project <name>

commands.name: agy --new-project <name>
commands.summary: Creates a new project at startup.
commands.what: agy --new-project <name> (since 1.0.12) creates a new project with the given name at startup. Without a name, the CLI has defaulted since 1.1.1 to naming newly initialized projects 'CLI Project'.

whenGood.title: Spin up a fresh project for a new task
whenGood.example: agy --new-project my-new-project

whenBad.title: You want to keep working in an already existing project
whenBad.why: --new-project always creates a new project instead of loading an existing one.
whenBad.alternative: Use agy --project <name>

commands.name: agy --sandbox
commands.summary: Forces sandbox isolation for terminal commands, including in print mode.
commands.what: agy --sandbox confines every local execution command the agent launches to OS-level containment: nsjail (Linux, kernel namespaces/cgroups), sandbox-exec (macOS, policy profiles), or AppContainer (Windows). Since 1.0.6 the flag is also correctly enforced in non-interactive print mode (-p/--print), not just interactively. The same setting can be persisted via enableTerminalSandbox in settings.json (default: false).

whenGood.title: Run untrusted or generated code in isolation
whenGood.example: Launch agy --sandbox in a new or unfamiliar repo

whenGood.title: Force sandbox isolation in scripts/CI as well
whenGood.example: agy --sandbox -p "<prompt>"

whenBad.title: The agent needs full access to system resources outside the workspace
whenBad.why: The sandbox deliberately restricts filesystem visibility and network access for terminal commands.
whenBad.alternative: Start agy without --sandbox, or confirm 'run without sandbox restrictions' per command

commands.name: agy --mode <default|accept-edits|plan>
commands.summary: Sets the agent execution mode at startup.
commands.what: agy --mode (since 1.1.0) sets the execution mode at startup: default (= request-review, pauses before file writes for a line-level diff preview to accept/reject), accept-edits, or plan. Without the flag, the same mode can be persisted permanently in the /settings panel under 'Agent Mode', or cycled during the session with shift+tab.

whenGood.title: Start directly in the lower-risk review mode
whenGood.example: agy --mode default

whenGood.title: Start directly in auto-accept mode when changes should apply without confirmation
whenGood.example: agy --mode accept-edits

whenBad.title: The mode should persist for all future launches, not just this one
whenBad.why: --mode only applies to the current launch.
whenBad.alternative: Set Agent Mode permanently in the /settings panel

commands.name: agy --dangerously-skip-permissions
commands.summary: Bypasses permission restrictions at startup (launch flag).
commands.what: agy --dangerously-skip-permissions is documented as a launch flag that can bypass certain safety restrictions of the permission system; the settings panel shows where the override came from. The flag is documented only on the older docs/cli-using page, not in the current core reference table.

whenGood.title: Avoid per-tool-call confirmations in a deliberately isolated, trusted environment
whenGood.example: Use agy --dangerously-skip-permissions only in a throwaway/sandbox environment, never on a production system

whenBad.title: You're working on a normal project with sensitive data or production access
whenBad.why: As the name and docs indicate, the flag deliberately bypasses safety restrictions of the permission system.
whenBad.alternative: Use normal permission rules and grant individual rules selectively via /permissions

sources.title: Antigravity CLI docs: Using the CLI

commands.name: agy plugin list
commands.summary: Shows installed plugins and their loaded components.
commands.what: agy plugin list shows active plugin packages and the components they've loaded (e.g. skills, agents, MCP servers). Plugins follow the plugin.json schema with a required name field (alphanumeric, hyphens, underscores only) and an optional description field.

whenGood.title: Get an overview of currently installed plugins
whenGood.example: Run agy plugin list before a planned uninstall or update

whenBad.title: You want to enable/disable or remove a plugin
whenBad.why: list only shows the current state, it doesn't change anything.
whenBad.alternative: agy plugin enable/disable <name> or agy plugin uninstall <name>

commands.name: agy plugin install <path>
commands.summary: Installs a local or downloaded plugin directory.
commands.what: agy plugin install /path/to/local/plugin stages a plugin directory into your local profile. External plugin installs automatically resolve and initialize git submodules; installing directly from GitHub subpaths (with branch resolution) is also supported. The import copies the entire plugin directory, including non-skill subfolders like shared/.

whenGood.title: Wire in a locally developed plugin directory
whenGood.example: agy plugin install ./my-plugin

whenGood.title: Install a plugin straight from a GitHub repo or subpath
whenGood.example: agy plugin install github.com/org/repo/path/to/plugin

whenBad.title: You only want to bring over legacy Gemini CLI extensions
whenBad.why: plugin install expects a plugin in the native plugin.json format, not a Gemini CLI extension directly.
whenBad.alternative: Use agy plugin import gemini to convert extensions first

commands.name: agy plugin enable <name>
commands.summary: Reactivates a previously disabled plugin without reinstalling it.
commands.what: agy plugin enable <plugin_name> reactivates the tools of a plugin that was previously suspended with agy plugin disable, without needing to reinstall it.

whenGood.title: Make a suspended plugin usable again
whenGood.example: agy plugin enable my-plugin

whenBad.title: The plugin isn't installed yet
whenBad.why: enable only re-activates an already installed, disabled plugin.
whenBad.alternative: Use agy plugin install <path> first

commands.name: agy plugin disable <name>
commands.summary: Suspends a plugin without uninstalling it.
commands.what: agy plugin disable <plugin_name> suspends a plugin's tools without removing the plugin directory or its configuration. It can be reactivated at any time with agy plugin enable <name>.

whenGood.title: Switch a plugin off for testing without losing it
whenGood.example: agy plugin disable my-plugin to narrow down a conflict with another plugin

whenBad.title: The plugin should be removed permanently and completely
whenBad.why: disable leaves the directory and registry entries untouched.
whenBad.alternative: Use agy plugin uninstall <name>

commands.name: agy plugin uninstall <name>
commands.summary: Fully removes a plugin directory including its registry entries.
commands.what: agy plugin uninstall <plugin_name> purges the plugin directory completely and cleans up the associated registry entries.

whenGood.title: Permanently remove a plugin you no longer need
whenGood.example: agy plugin uninstall my-plugin

whenBad.title: The plugin should only be suspended temporarily
whenBad.why: uninstall fully removes the directory and registry entries; re-enabling it later requires reinstalling.
whenBad.alternative: Use agy plugin disable <name>

commands.name: agy plugin import gemini
commands.summary: Automatically converts legacy Gemini CLI extensions into native Antigravity CLI plugins.
commands.what: agy plugin import gemini scans local Gemini CLI directories, reads the extension manifests, and converts them into the native plugin layout. Legacy commands get converted to skills, existing skills are carried over, and mcpServers entries migrate into a dedicated mcp_config.json; for remote servers you then need to rename the url/httpUrl schema key to serverUrl manually. The output lists per extension exactly what was converted, e.g. 'commands: 4 legacy commands converted to skills'.

whenGood.title: Automatically bring over existing extensions when switching from Gemini CLI
whenGood.example: Run agy plugin import gemini right after installing Antigravity CLI

whenBad.title: You want to install a single, brand-new plugin from scratch
whenBad.why: import gemini only converts existing legacy Gemini CLI extensions, not new plugins.
whenBad.alternative: Use agy plugin install <path>

sources.title: Antigravity CLI docs: Gemini CLI Migration

commands.name: install.sh --skip-aliases
commands.summary: Skips cleaning up old agy/antigravity shell aliases during install.
commands.what: The --skip-aliases flag is passed when running the install script (install.sh/install.ps1/install.cmd) and stops the script from cleaning up or updating existing legacy agy or antigravity shell aliases in your shell profile.

whenGood.title: Keep your own, manually maintained agy/antigravity shell aliases
whenGood.example: curl -fsSL https://antigravity.google/cli/install.sh | bash -s -- --skip-aliases

whenBad.title: Old, broken, or wrongly-pointed aliases are still in your shell profile
whenBad.why: With --skip-aliases, the installer specifically won't clean up those old aliases automatically.
whenBad.alternative: Run the installer without --skip-aliases so old aliases get cleaned up automatically

commands.name: install.sh --skip-path
commands.summary: Skips automatically appending PATH in your shell profile during install.
commands.what: The --skip-path flag is passed when running the install script and stops it from automatically appending the binary directory (~/.local/bin or %LOCALAPPDATA%\agy\bin) to PATH in your shell profile.

whenGood.title: Manage the PATH entry yourself, e.g. in an existing dotfiles setup
whenGood.example: curl -fsSL https://antigravity.google/cli/install.sh | bash -s -- --skip-path

whenBad.title: agy should be callable from any terminal right after installing, with no extra manual step
whenBad.why: Without the automatic PATH entry, the shell won't find the agy binary without a manual PATH change.
whenBad.alternative: Run the installer without --skip-path

commands.name: @<path>
commands.summary: Opens an interactive overlay for picking a file path right in the prompt box.
commands.what: Typing @ in the prompt box opens the Interactive Path Suggestion overlay: it lets you browse workspace file paths and insert one directly into your prompt as an absolute path, helping the agent search the code more precisely. Since 1.0.6, @ triggers suggestions unconditionally, including right after an opening parenthesis.

whenGood.title: Reference a specific file precisely without typing the path by hand
whenGood.example: Type @src/auth/ to browse to the right file in the overlay

whenBad.title: You want to run a terminal command directly
whenBad.why: @ only inserts a file path into the prompt, it doesn't execute anything.
whenBad.alternative: Use ! at the start of a line for direct terminal commands

sources.title: Antigravity CLI docs: Using the CLI

commands.name: !<shell-command>
commands.summary: Runs a terminal command directly from the prompt box.
commands.what: A ! at the start of your input runs the rest of the line as a shell command directly, without involving the agent.

whenGood.title: Quickly run a terminal command without leaving the TUI
whenGood.example: !git status

whenBad.title: You want the agent to choose the command itself or interpret the result
whenBad.why: ! runs the command directly and unmodified, with no agent involvement.
whenBad.alternative: Phrase the request as a normal prompt so the agent suggests the right command itself

sources.title: Antigravity CLI docs: Using the CLI

commands.summary: Shows help and a list of all slash commands.
commands.what: Typing ? in the prompt box opens help with a list of all available slash commands.

whenGood.title: Quickly check which slash commands are available
whenGood.example: Type ? in the prompt box
