whenBad.title: You already know the command and want to run it directly
whenBad.why: ? only opens the overview, it doesn't execute anything itself.
whenBad.alternative: Type the known slash command directly

sources.title: Antigravity CLI docs: Using the CLI

commands.name: esc esc
commands.summary: Clears the prompt box, as long as no response is currently streaming.
commands.what: Pressing esc twice clears the current input in the prompt box, as long as no response stream is currently active.

whenGood.title: Quickly discard a wrong or no-longer-needed input
whenGood.example: Press esc esc to clear what you've typed so far

whenBad.title: You want to cancel a running agent response stream
whenBad.why: esc esc only clears the prompt box; ctrl+c is documented for interrupting an active stream.
whenBad.alternative: Use ctrl+c to interrupt an active agent run

sources.title: Antigravity CLI docs: Using the CLI

commands.name: .agents/skills/*.md → /<skill-name>
commands.summary: Local or global markdown skills automatically become their own /<skill-name> slash commands.
commands.what: A markdown file with frontmatter (name, description) plus agent instructions in .agents/skills/ at the workspace root gets compiled the next time agy starts and is then available as /<skill-name> in the prompt box. The same applies globally to markdown files in ~/.gemini/antigravity-cli/skills/ — these are automatically imported as a global slash command across all workspaces. Migrating from Gemini CLI changes the paths: workspace-local moves from .gemini/skills/ to .agents/skills/, global moves from ~/.gemini/skills/ to ~/.gemini/antigravity-cli/skills/.

whenGood.title: Turn a recurring workflow into its own command for the project
whenGood.example: Add format-tests.md with frontmatter to .agents/skills/ so /format-tests shows up in the prompt box

whenGood.title: Use a skill across every project
whenGood.example: Put the skill file in ~/.gemini/antigravity-cli/skills/ instead

whenBad.title: Skills migrated from Gemini CLI still sit at the old path (.gemini/skills/ or ~/.gemini/skills/)
whenBad.why: Antigravity CLI only recognizes skills at the new paths (.agents/skills/ or ~/.gemini/antigravity-cli/skills/) — the old paths are no longer picked up.
whenBad.alternative: Move the skill files to the new path manually (agy plugin import gemini only converts extensions, not loose skill files)

sources.title: Antigravity CLI docs: Gemini CLI Migration

commands.name: /add-dir
commands.summary: Adds an extra directory to the active workspace.
commands.what: /add-dir belongs to the Utilities category of the central Antigravity CLI slash command reference. It extends the active workspace with another directory so the agent can access files outside the starting directory. It takes a path as an argument.

whenGood.title: Bring in code from a second repo or folder
whenGood.example: /add-dir ../shared-lib

whenGood.title: Add a monorepo sub-project to the session
whenGood.example: /add-dir packages/api

whenBad.title: You only need to reference a file once
whenBad.why: /add-dir permanently attaches an entire directory to the workspace; for a one-off reference, a file path in the prompt is enough.
whenBad.alternative: Use @<path> directly in the prompt

commands.name: /agents
commands.summary: Opens the agent manager panel to pick custom agents and monitor running subagents.
commands.what: /agents belongs to the Tools & Tasks category. In the panel, ↑/↓ navigate the list, Enter selects a custom agent or opens a subagent's detail view, k kills an active subagent, a approves and d denies inline tool requests right from the list, and Esc goes back. Complements the --agent startup flag and the agy agents subcommand.

whenGood.title: Switch between multiple custom agents
whenGood.example: Open /agents and use ↑/↓ + Enter to pick a different agent for the session

whenGood.title: Check on or cancel a running subagent task
whenGood.example: Open /agents, select a subagent, and kill it with k

whenBad.title: You just want a quick list of available custom agents
whenBad.why: /agents opens an interactive panel; for a plain list without panel interaction, the subcommand is enough.
whenBad.alternative: Run agy agents at startup

commands.name: /artifact
commands.summary: Opens the artifact review panel.
commands.what: /artifact belongs to the Tools & Tasks category of the slash command reference and opens a dedicated panel for reviewing artifacts the agent produced during the session.

whenGood.title: Review artifacts the agent generated
whenGood.example: Open /artifact after a task to check the generated outputs

whenBad.title: You need a plain code diff, not artifacts
whenBad.why: /artifact shows artifacts, not a line-level diff of code changes.
whenBad.alternative: /diff

commands.name: /btw
commands.summary: Asks a side question in the background without interrupting the main conversation.
commands.what: /btw belongs to the Utilities category. It fires off an extra question as a background task while the running main conversation continues undisturbed — handy for short side questions that shouldn't eat into the main thread's current context.

whenGood.title: Ask a quick side question without interrupting the main task
whenGood.example: /btw what does this function in utils.py do?

whenBad.title: The question is actually part of the main task
whenBad.why: /btw runs separately from the main thread in the background — not suited for questions meant to shape how the main task continues.
whenBad.alternative: Ask the question directly in the main prompt

commands.name: /clear
commands.summary: Clears the terminal and active conversation contexts. Alias: /new.
commands.what: /clear belongs to the Utilities category, alias /new. It resets both the terminal display and the active conversation contexts — for a clean restart within the same session.

whenGood.title: Start a new topic within the same session
whenGood.example: /clear after finishing a task, before starting a completely new topic

whenBad.title: You want to keep the current conversation and come back to it later
whenBad.why: /clear wipes the active context — mid-task you lose the thread's working state.
whenBad.alternative: Use /resume to switch between threads instead of clearing

commands.name: /config
commands.summary: Opens the interactive settings editor. Alias: /settings.
commands.what: /config belongs to the Configurations category, alias /settings. It opens an interactive editor for the CLI's settings.json configuration, covering things like rendering mode and terminal behavior.

whenGood.title: Change CLI settings without manually editing settings.json
whenGood.example: Open /config and adjust a setting directly in the panel

whenBad.title: You just want to switch the reasoning model
whenBad.why: /config covers general configuration; there's a dedicated, more targeted command for picking the model.
whenBad.alternative: /model

commands.name: /context
commands.summary: Opens the context usage visualization.
commands.what: /context belongs to the Utilities category and visually shows how much of the available context window is already used up.

whenGood.title: Check remaining context capacity before a long task
whenGood.example: Call /context to see how much room is left in the context window

whenBad.title: You want to check model quota (requests per period), not context window usage
whenBad.why: /context shows context window usage, not the model's usage quota.
whenBad.alternative: /usage

commands.name: /copy
commands.summary: Copies the last agent response to the clipboard.
commands.what: /copy belongs to the Utilities category and puts the text of the agent's most recent response onto the system clipboard.

whenGood.title: Quickly paste a response into another application
whenGood.example: /copy after a code explanation, to paste it into a note

whenBad.title: You want a full code diff, not a plain text response
whenBad.why: /copy copies the last text response, not a structured code diff.
whenBad.alternative: Open /diff and view the changes there

commands.name: /credits
commands.summary: Shows remaining G1 credits and purchase links.
commands.what: /credits belongs to the Account category and shows the current G1 credits balance for CLI usage plus links to buy more. Together with the useG1Credits setting, this controls billing for requests beyond the plan's included quota.

whenGood.title: Check the remaining balance before a bigger task
whenGood.example: Call /credits before starting a compute-heavy session

whenBad.title: You want to check model usage quota, not the credits balance
whenBad.why: /credits shows the G1 credits balance; /usage shows the plan's general model quota.
whenBad.alternative: /usage

commands.name: /diff
commands.summary: Opens the interactive diff viewer with three modes (VCS/Turn/Commit).
commands.what: /diff belongs to the Utilities category. Tab, ← or → cycle through VCS, Turn, and Commit mode; c adds a comment on the current line, d deletes it, n/N jump between hunks, and Shift+Y sends the comments you've set to the agent while Shift+N discards them.

whenGood.title: Review the changes from a single turn
whenGood.example: Open /diff, switch to Turn mode with Tab, and check the latest change

whenGood.title: Send inline comments on individual hunks back to the agent
whenGood.example: Open /diff, add a comment to a line with c, and send it with Shift+Y

whenBad.title: You want an assessment of the changes, not just the raw diff text
whenBad.why: /diff only displays the diff text — it doesn't evaluate it.
whenBad.alternative: Ask the agent directly in the prompt to summarize or assess the changes

commands.name: /exit
commands.summary: Ends the TUI session and restores the host shell. Alias: /quit.
commands.what: /exit belongs to the Core category, alias /quit. It ends the running Antigravity CLI TUI session and hands control back to the original shell.

whenGood.title: Cleanly end the session
whenGood.example: /exit once the task is done

whenBad.title: You just want to clear the current context, keeping the session running
whenBad.why: /exit ends the entire TUI session, not just the conversation context.
whenBad.alternative: /clear

commands.name: /fast
commands.summary: Turns on fast mode, which skips reasoning plans for quick actions.
commands.what: According to the central slash command reference, /fast belongs to the Configurations category and enables a sped-up mode where the agent skips reasoning plans before acting, so it responds faster.

whenGood.title: Run a simple, clearly-defined action without lengthy planning
whenGood.example: Turn on /fast before requesting a simple file rename

whenBad.title: A complex task with several dependencies
whenBad.why: Without a reasoning plan, complex tasks carry a higher risk of incomplete or incorrect steps.
whenBad.alternative: Leave /fast off, or use /planning for multi-step tasks

commands.name: /feedback
commands.summary: Opens the feedback panel.
commands.what: /feedback belongs to the Utilities category and opens a panel for sending feedback about the CLI directly to the Antigravity team.

whenGood.title: Report a bug or suggestion directly from the CLI
whenGood.example: Open /feedback and enter what you noticed

whenBad.title: A technical problem that needs solving right now
whenBad.why: /feedback sends a message to the team but doesn't fix an acute problem in the running session.
whenBad.alternative: Check /help or the troubleshooting docs

commands.name: /fork
commands.summary: Clones the current conversation thread into a parallel session. Alias: /branch.
commands.what: /fork belongs to the Conversations category, alias /branch. It duplicates the current thread, including context, into a new, parallel-running session, so different approaches can be pursued independently of each other.

whenGood.title: Try two approaches in parallel
whenGood.example: /fork to test an alternative implementation without losing the main thread

whenBad.title: You just want to go back to an earlier point in the same thread
whenBad.why: /fork creates a new parallel thread; it doesn't change the existing one.
whenBad.alternative: /rewind

commands.name: /help
commands.summary: Opens the help panel with commands and shortcuts.
commands.what: /help belongs to the Utilities category and shows an overview of all available slash commands and keyboard shortcuts, right in the terminal.

whenGood.title: Look up the available commands
whenGood.example: Call /help to see the list of all slash commands

whenBad.title: You need the detailed shortcuts of a specific panel like /diff or /agents
whenBad.why: /help shows a general overview, not the detailed shortcuts of individual panels.
whenBad.alternative: Open the relevant panel itself, e.g. /diff or /agents

commands.name: /hooks
commands.summary: Browses the active pre-/post-hooks.
commands.what: /hooks belongs to the Tools & Tasks category and opens a searchable overview of the currently active pre- and post-hooks, such as those installed via plugins.

whenGood.title: Check which hooks are currently active
whenGood.example: Open /hooks to see which plugin registers a particular hook

whenBad.title: You want to fully enable/disable a plugin, not just view hooks
whenBad.why: /hooks only shows an overview; it doesn't manage plugins.
whenBad.alternative: agy plugin enable/disable <name>

commands.name: /keybindings
commands.summary: Opens the interactive keybinding editor.
commands.what: /keybindings belongs to the Configurations category and opens an editor for customizing the CLI's keyboard shortcuts, which are stored in a separate keybindings file.

whenGood.title: Adjust keyboard shortcuts to your own habits
whenGood.example: Open /keybindings and change the shortcut for a frequently used command

whenBad.title: You want to change general settings, not shortcuts
whenBad.why: /keybindings only covers keyboard shortcuts, not the rest of the configuration.
whenBad.alternative: /config

commands.name: /logout
commands.summary: Disconnects the profile and deletes the auth token from the keyring.
commands.what: /logout belongs to the Account category and signs out the connected Google account by removing the stored auth token from the OS keyring (Keychain/Secret Service/Credential Manager). The next start requires a fresh browser or URL+code auth flow.

whenGood.title: Switch accounts
whenGood.example: /logout, then restart agy and sign in with a different Google account

whenBad.title: You just want to end the current session, keeping the account connected
whenBad.why: /logout removes the auth token entirely; for a plain end of session a simple session exit is enough.
whenBad.alternative: /exit

commands.name: /mcp
commands.summary: Opens the MCP server manager.
commands.what: /mcp belongs to the Tools & Tasks category and opens a panel for managing connected MCP servers (stdio and remote via serverUrl), including connection status. Configuration lives separately in a global file (~/.gemini/config/mcp_config.json) and a workspace file (.agents/mcp_config.json).

whenGood.title: Check connected MCP servers and their status
whenGood.example: Open /mcp to see whether a server is connected

whenBad.title: You want to set a permission for a single MCP tool
whenBad.why: /mcp manages server connections; the granular mcp(server/tool) rules are handled through permissions management.
whenBad.alternative: /permissions

commands.name: /model
commands.summary: Picks the reasoning model, persisting across sessions.
commands.what: /model belongs to the Configurations category. The choice applies beyond the current session until changed again. Complements the --model startup flag and the agy models subcommand, which lists available models from the Shared Agent Engine (including Gemini 3.1 Pro/3.5 Flash on all plans, and Claude Sonnet/Opus 4.6 plus GPT-OSS-120b on Google AI Ultra only).

whenGood.title: Pick a stronger reasoning model for a more demanding task
whenGood.example: Open /model and switch to a more capable model

whenBad.title: You only want the model set for the next start, not permanently
whenBad.why: /model changes the choice persistently across sessions.
whenBad.alternative: Use agy --model <name> at startup

commands.name: /open
commands.summary: Opens a path in the system default editor.
commands.what: /open belongs to the Utilities category and opens the given file or path in the system's default editor, outside the CLI.

whenGood.title: Open a file for manual editing outside the CLI
whenGood.example: /open src/index.ts

whenBad.title: You just want to reference a file path in the prompt
whenBad.why: /open launches the external editor; that's unnecessary for a plain prompt reference.
whenBad.alternative: Use @<path> in the prompt

commands.name: /permissions
commands.summary: Opens the interactive tool permissions manager.
commands.what: /permissions belongs to the Configurations category. A scope picker lets you choose between Project, Shared, and Global, then between the rule viewer tabs allow/deny/ask; a adds a rule, e or Ctrl+G edits it, d or Backspace deletes it. Rules follow the format action(target), e.g. command(git) or mcp(server/tool).

whenGood.title: Allow specific commands to run automatically
whenGood.example: Open /permissions, pick the Project scope, and set command(git) to allow

whenGood.title: Block a specific MCP tool
whenGood.example: Open /permissions and set mcp(server/tool) to deny

whenBad.title: You want to bypass permissions entirely for a single start
whenBad.why: /permissions manages persistent rules, not a one-time skip for a single start.
whenBad.alternative: agy --dangerously-skip-permissions at startup (use with caution)

commands.name: /planning
commands.summary: Turns on the multi-turn plan generation mode.
commands.what: According to the central slash command reference, /planning belongs to the Configurations category and enables a mode where the agent generates a plan across multiple turns before executing, instead of acting directly.

whenGood.title: Let a complex task with several dependencies be planned properly
whenGood.example: Turn on /planning before starting a larger refactoring task

whenBad.title: A small, unambiguous single action
whenBad.why: For trivial actions, planning mode adds unnecessary overhead.
whenBad.alternative: /fast for quick, simple actions

commands.name: /rename
commands.summary: Renames the current session thread.
commands.what: /rename belongs to the Conversations category and assigns a new name to the current conversation thread, making it easier to find again later in the conversation picker.

whenGood.title: Give a thread a clear name before switching to a different topic
whenGood.example: /rename Migration-Auth-Refactor

whenBad.title: You want to rename a saved thread without it being active right now
whenBad.why: /rename acts on the currently active thread; saved threads have a rename option inside the resume picker.
whenBad.alternative: Open /resume and rename it there with F2

commands.name: /resume
commands.summary: Opens the conversation picker to load earlier threads. Aliases: /switch, /conversation.
commands.what: /resume belongs to the Conversations category, aliases /switch and /conversation. In the picker, F2 renames the selected thread, Ctrl+Delete deletes it, and Tab switches between the CLI tab and the Antigravity 2.0 tab to import running desktop conversations from the GUI. Complements the -c/--continue and --conversation <id> startup flags.

whenGood.title: Return to an earlier thread
whenGood.example: Open /resume and pick an earlier thread from the list

whenGood.title: Import a conversation started in Antigravity 2.0 (desktop GUI) into the CLI
whenGood.example: Open /resume and switch to the Antigravity tab with Tab

whenBad.title: You just want to continue the last conversation directly at startup, without a picker
whenBad.why: /resume opens an interactive panel; for a direct restart without picking, the startup flag is enough.
whenBad.alternative: agy -c / --continue at startup

commands.name: /rewind
commands.summary: Rolls the conversation history back to a previous checkpoint. Alias: /undo.
commands.what: /rewind belongs to the Conversations category, alias /undo. It resets the conversation history to an earlier state within the same thread, instead of creating a new thread.

whenGood.title: Undo a failed intermediate step
whenGood.example: /rewind to return to the state before a faulty change

whenBad.title: You want to try an alternative approach alongside the existing history
whenBad.why: /rewind overwrites the history from the chosen point onward, instead of keeping it alongside a new path.
whenBad.alternative: /fork

commands.name: /skills
commands.summary: Browses loaded local and global Agent Skills.
commands.what: /skills belongs to the Tools & Tasks category and shows the currently loaded skills from the workspace (.agents/skills/*.md) and the global directory (~/.gemini/antigravity-cli/skills/). Every markdown skill file automatically becomes its own /<skill-name> command.

whenGood.title: Check which skills are currently available
whenGood.example: Open /skills to see whether a newly added skill file was picked up

whenBad.title: You want to fully install or uninstall a plugin
whenBad.why: /skills only shows the loaded skills; it doesn't manage plugins.
whenBad.alternative: agy plugin install/uninstall

commands.name: /statusline
commands.summary: Opens statusline customization.
commands.what: /statusline belongs to the Configurations category. Without an argument it toggles the statusline on/off; on/enable turns it on, off/disable turns it off, a passed shell command sets a custom renderer, reset resets the statusline, and help shows the options.

whenGood.title: Turn the statusline on or off
whenGood.example: /statusline off

whenGood.title: Set up a custom renderer via a shell command
whenGood.example: /statusline <shell-command> for a custom status display

whenBad.title: You want to change the terminal window title, not the statusline
whenBad.why: /statusline affects the statusline inside the TUI, not the terminal's window title.
whenBad.alternative: /title

commands.name: /tasks
commands.summary: Opens the task manager panel for monitoring background shell logs.
commands.what: /tasks belongs to the Tools & Tasks category and shows running and completed background tasks along with their shell logs in a dedicated panel.

whenGood.title: Check the log of a command running in the background
whenGood.example: Open /tasks to check the output of a longer-running background shell task

whenBad.title: You want to monitor subagent status, not background shell tasks
whenBad.why: /tasks shows shell task logs; subagents have their own panel.
whenBad.alternative: /agents

commands.name: /title
commands.summary: Toggles terminal window title updates.
commands.what: /title belongs to the Configurations category and controls whether the CLI automatically updates the terminal emulator's window title. Calling it without an argument toggles the state, on/off set it explicitly.

whenGood.title: Turn off window title updates specifically
whenGood.example: /title off

whenBad.title: You want to change the statusline content inside the TUI
whenBad.why: /title only affects the terminal emulator's window title, not the statusline inside the TUI.
whenBad.alternative: /statusline

commands.name: /usage
commands.summary: Shows model quota usage. Alias: /quota.
commands.what: /usage belongs to the Utilities category, alias /quota, and shows how much of the current plan's quota (Standard/Pro/Ultra) has already been used.

whenGood.title: Check remaining requests in the current time window
whenGood.example: Call /usage before starting a large batch of requests

whenBad.title: You want to check the G1 credits balance, not general model quota
whenBad.why: /usage shows plan quota; the separate credits balance has its own command.
whenBad.alternative: /credits

commands.name: /bug
commands.summary: Reports a bug or shares your conversation.
commands.what:
Reports a bug or shares your conversation.

You choose how much session history to include and confirm on a consent screen first. Signed in to Anthropic on a first-party connection, the report goes to Anthropic; on a third-party provider or without Anthropic credentials, Claude Code writes it to a local archive under ~/.claude/feedback-bundles/ that you forward yourself. Alias: /share. Before v2.1.212, /bug and /share were aliases of /feedback.

whenGood.title: Report a bug with context to Anthropic
whenGood.example: /bug

whenGood.title: Without an Anthropic login: write the report locally and forward it yourself
whenGood.example: /bug report

whenBad.title: Not a bug but a product idea or general feedback
whenBad.why: /bug is built for bug reports and sharing a session.
whenBad.alternative: /feedback

commands.name: /stop
commands.summary: Stops the current background session.
commands.what:
Stops the current background session.

Only available while attached to a background session; the transcript and any worktree are kept. To detach without stopping, use /exit.

whenGood.title: End a session running in the background
whenGood.example: /stop

whenBad.title: Only detach but keep the session running
whenBad.why: /stop ends the session; the transcript and worktree are kept, but the work does not continue.
whenBad.alternative: /exit

commands.name: /team-onboarding
commands.summary: Generates a team onboarding guide from your usage history.
commands.what:
Generates a team onboarding guide from your usage history.

Claude analyzes your sessions, commands, and MCP server usage from the past 30 days and produces a markdown guide a teammate can paste as a first message to get set up quickly. For claude.ai subscribers on Pro, Max, Team, and Enterprise plans, it also returns a share link.

whenGood.title: Give new teammates a grounded starting point
whenGood.example: /team-onboarding

whenBad.title: A fresh project with no usage history of your own
whenBad.why: the guide draws on your past 30 days; without history it stays thin.
whenBad.alternative: /init

commands.name: /terminal-setup
commands.summary: Configures terminal keybindings such as Shift+Enter.
commands.what:
Configures terminal keybindings for Shift+Enter and other shortcuts.

Only visible in terminals that need it, like VS Code, Cursor, Devin Desktop, Alacritty, or Zed.

whenGood.title: Enable Shift+Enter in the VS Code or Cursor terminal
whenGood.example: /terminal-setup

whenBad.title: A terminal that already handles the shortcuts natively
whenBad.why: the command is not even shown there, because no setup is needed.
whenBad.alternative: do nothing — the shortcuts already work

commands.name: /theme
commands.summary: Changes the color theme.
commands.what:
Changes the color theme.

Includes an auto option that matches your terminal’s light or dark background, light and dark variants, colorblind-accessible (daltonized) themes, ANSI themes that use your terminal’s color palette, and any custom themes from ~/.claude/themes/ or plugins. Select "New custom theme…" to create one.

whenGood.title: Switch to a light or dark theme
whenGood.example: /theme

whenGood.title: Pick a colorblind-accessible theme
whenGood.example: /theme

whenBad.title: Change the terminal font or size
whenBad.why: /theme only controls Claude Code’s colors, not the terminal’s own rendering.
whenBad.alternative: use your terminal app’s settings

commands.name: /tui
commands.summary: Sets the terminal renderer and relaunches into it.
commands.what:
Sets the terminal UI renderer and relaunches into it with your conversation intact.

fullscreen enables the flicker-free alt-screen renderer. With no argument, it prints the active renderer.

whenGood.title: Switch to the flicker-free fullscreen renderer
whenGood.example: /tui fullscreen

whenGood.title: Check which renderer is currently active
whenGood.example: /tui

whenBad.title: Expecting the conversation to be cleared
whenBad.why: the switch keeps your conversation; the command does not reset it.
whenBad.alternative: /clear

commands.name: /ultrareview
commands.summary: Runs a deep, multi-agent code review in a cloud sandbox.
commands.what:
Runs a deep, multi-agent code review in a cloud sandbox.

Pass a PR reference to review that pull request, or a branch name to change the comparison base. The preferred invocation is now /code-review ultra, and /ultrareview remains as an alias. Includes 3 free runs on Pro and Max, then requires usage credits.

whenGood.title: Have a pull request reviewed thoroughly
whenGood.example: /ultrareview 42

whenGood.title: Compare against a different base branch
whenGood.example: /ultrareview main

whenBad.title: A quick local look at a small change
whenBad.why: /ultrareview launches a heavy cloud review; that is overkill for a quick glance.
whenBad.alternative: ask Claude directly to look over the local diff

commands.name: /usage-credits
commands.summary: Configures usage credits or requests them from your admin.
commands.what:
Configures usage credits, or requests them from your admin, when you hit a limit.

On Pro and Max plans, opens an in-CLI dialog to buy usage credits, set a monthly spend limit, and configure auto-reload. On Claude Code versions before v2.1.207 and on other plans, it opens the usage-credits billing page in your browser instead. Team and Enterprise members without billing access send a usage-credits request to their admin after confirming in a dialog.

whenGood.title: Buy more credits after hitting the limit (Pro/Max)
whenGood.example: /usage-credits

whenGood.title: Without billing access, request credits from your admin
whenGood.example: /usage-credits

whenBad.title: Only view current usage
whenBad.why: /usage-credits manages credits; it does not show the usage overview.
whenBad.alternative: /usage

commands.name: /voice
commands.summary: Toggles voice dictation on or off.
commands.what:
Toggles voice dictation on or off, or enables it in a specific mode.

hold dictates while you hold the key; tap toggles with a tap; off turns it off. Requires a Claude.ai account.

whenGood.title: Speak into the terminal instead of typing
whenGood.example: /voice tap

whenGood.title: Dictate only while holding a key
whenGood.example: /voice hold

whenBad.title: Without a Claude.ai account
whenBad.why: voice dictation requires a Claude.ai account.
whenBad.alternative: keep typing on the keyboard

commands.name: /web-setup
commands.summary: Connects your GitHub account to Claude Code on the web.
commands.what: Connects your GitHub account to Claude Code on the web using your local gh CLI credentials.

whenGood.title: Link Claude Code on the web to your GitHub account
whenGood.example: /web-setup

whenBad.title: Without the gh CLI installed and signed in
whenBad.why: the command uses your local gh CLI credentials; without them it cannot connect anything.
whenBad.alternative: install the gh CLI first and sign in with "gh auth login"

commands.name: /workflows
commands.summary: Opens the progress view for running workflows.
commands.what: Opens the workflow progress view to watch, pause, resume, or save running and completed workflows.

whenGood.title: Watch or pause a running workflow
whenGood.example: /workflows

whenGood.title: Save a completed workflow to reuse later
whenGood.example: /workflows

whenBad.title: No workflow is running at all
whenBad.why: the view shows running and completed workflows; without one it stays empty.
whenBad.alternative: start a workflow first

commands.name: /pr-comments
commands.summary: No longer exists — removed in version 2.1.91.
commands.what:
No longer exists — removed in version 2.1.91.

Instead of a dedicated command, you now ask Claude directly to view a pull request’s comments. On earlier versions, /pr-comments fetched a GitHub pull request’s comments (auto-detecting the current branch’s PR, or taking a PR URL or number) and required the gh CLI.

whenGood.title: Nothing — the command is gone
whenGood.example: Ask Claude directly: "Look at the comments on PR 42."

whenBad.title: On version 2.1.91 or later
whenBad.why: the command was removed in 2.1.91 and does nothing.
whenBad.alternative: ask Claude directly for the PR comments
